Instead, it shows that infected ticks have higher mortality after exposure to low temperatures (4°C), indicating reduced survival in cold conditions. 252.Option: The addition of Quil A significantly (P<0.05) increased IgG2c but not significantly IgG1 titers by ∼5–10 fold (Fig. 5B–C). In summary, a single 1 µg dose of insect-cell derived CHIKV VLPs induced potent CHIKV-specific neutralizing antibody and IgG titers.
Evidence: The addition of Quil A significantly (P<0.05) increased IgG2c but not significantly IgG1 titers by ∼5–10 fold (Fig. 5B–C).

253.Rationale: The passage confirms that reinfections are common for H. ducreyi genital ulcers, but it provides no evidence comparing reinfection rates between children and adults. The claim that reinfection is more common in children than adults cannot be derived from the passage.

Evidence: Flies (n = 10) were caught outside 10 houses of patients with ulcers; 9 (90%) of 10 flies had H. ducreyi DNA and 5 (50%) had amplifiable T. p. pertenue DNA.

Nevertheless, there is no clear evidence of H. ducreyi or yaws transmission by insects or fomites.

The claim that they are the primary source of new infections is not supported by epidemiological evidence in the passage; it overinterprets the DNA findings without proof of transmission.

If H. ducreyi adheres and survives on the healthy skin of asymptomatic carriers, this could enhance its persistence in the community and its transmission, because skin-colonizing bacteria might escape systemic azithromycin treatment.
Overall, 16 (21%, 95%CI 12–31) of 78 asymptomatic participants had H. ducreyi bacterial DNA on the skin.
Flies (n = 10) were caught outside 10 houses of patients with ulcers; 9 (90%) of 10 flies had H. ducreyi DNA.
Environmental contamination was observed in 3 (50%) of 6 bed sheets sampled from 6 households with index cases; 2 sheets had detectable H. ducreyi DNA.
253.Rationale: The passage directly discusses asymptomatic skin colonization as a potential reservoir, provides evidence of H. ducreyi DNA on asymptomatic skin and in environmental sources (flies and bed linens), and suggests that skin-colonizing bacteria may escape systemic azithromycin treatment. Together, these factors logically explain the persistence of H. ducreyi after MDA, especially in contrast to T. p. pertenue, which was reduced by MDA.

Both infections are thought to be exclusively transmitted through non-intact skin contact with infectious lesions.
253.Rationale: The passage shows a high prevalence of H. ducreyi DNA on the skin of asymptomatic children, but it explicitly states that transmission is through non-intact skin contact, not sexual contact. The inference that it is more transmissible through sexual contact contradicts the passage and is not supported by any evidence provided.

253.Option: The limited impact of azithromycin on H. ducreyi is likely due to widespread resistance to macrolides in the H. ducreyi population.
Evidence: Cutaneous strains of H. ducreyi are also susceptible to macrolides in vitro [11].
253.Rationale: The passage indicates that H. ducreyi strains are susceptible to macrolides in vitro, with no mention of widespread resistance. The limited impact of azithromycin is attributed to other factors such as reservoirs and escape from treatment, not antibiotic resistance.

254.Option: The frequent and prolonged interactions between humans and bats in buildings, combined with common attempts to remove bats, create repeated opportunities for pathogen exposure through both direct and indirect contact, as well as through domestic animals that may act as intermediate hosts.
Evidence: Over 70% of people reported bat use of their buildings for >5 years
Most properties (88%) had bat presence year-round.

Survey participants described frequent exposure to bats that would support pathogen transmission through two main routes: direct and indirect (fecal/oral) contact, with indirect contact between bats and humans reported more frequently than direct contacts
Close to half of participants (39%) reported direct contact with bats
Over 90% of respondents reported indirect contact with bats, mostly through interactions with their feces and urine
Attempts to remove bats from buildings were reported by almost 80% of participants
Of those reporting removal efforts, almost half reported direct contact with bats.
13 respondents reported seeing domestic animals (dogs, cats, and chickens) consume dead bats on their property
Domestic animals can also be exposed to these pathogens after contact with bats and their excreta or fluids
254.Rationale: The passage provides direct evidence of prolonged bat presence in buildings ( >5 years, year-round), frequent direct and indirect contact between humans and bats, and common removal attempts that often involve direct contact. Additionally, domestic animals are reported to consume dead bats and are noted as potentially exposed to bat pathogens. These factors collectively create repeated opportunities for pathogen exposure through multiple routes, including via domestic animals that could serve as intermediate hosts, which is a key epidemiological dynamic for zoonotic spillover.

254.Option: The high frequency of indirect contact with bat excreta and the common practice of disposing of dead bats by throwing them over property lines suggest that bat-borne pathogens are likely to spread widely within communities through environmental contamination.
Evidence: Over 90% of respondents reported indirect contact with bats, mostly through interactions with their feces and urine
Daily occurrences of indirect contact were reported by most participants (78%)
Nearly 65% reported dead bats on properties (n = 51). Most removed dead bats, usually by throwing them over property lines (n = 30) or swept them outside (n = 7).
Bats can harbor zoonotic pathogens that may be shed in excreta and bodily fluids (eg., feces, urine, saliva, blood, etc.)
254.Rationale: The passage demonstrates a high frequency of indirect contact with bat excreta (daily for most participants), indicating ongoing exposure to potentially contaminated materials. The disposal of dead bats by throwing them over property lines is reported as the most common method, which can introduce pathogens into the shared environment. Given that bat excreta and carcasses can harbor zoonotic pathogens, these practices create pathways for environmental contamination that could extend beyond individual properties, facilitating community-wide spread of bat-borne pathogens—an inference consistent with epidemiological principles of environmental transmission in zoonotic spillover.

255.Option: The use of household water treatment methods such as boiling significantly reduces the risk of infection from Cryptosporidium and Giardia in drinking water, as demonstrated by the 6 log10 reduction in viable parasites.
Evidence: we used WHO reported [39] 6 log10 reduction values for Cryptosporidium and Giardia from boiling (i.e. 6 log10 = 99.9999% reduction)

255.Rationale: While boiling reduces parasite viability, the sensitivity analysis shows that the fraction of the population boiling water is among the least sensitive parameters, meaning it does not significantly influence variation in estimated disease burden compared to other factors.

255.Option: The spatial proximity of tubewells to household latrines is the main determinant of whether detected parasites are from human or non-human sources, directly influencing disease burden estimates.
255.Rationale: The passage does not mention spatial proximity of tubewells to latrines. The study uses three scenarios (HUM, ENV, MST) based on environmental loading and microbial source tracking to estimate the fraction of parasites infectious-to-humans, not spatial proximity.

Evidence: A two-sample Kolmogorov-Smirnov test indicated concentrations were significantly different between years (P-value = < 0.001).
Comparing years, 2012 had a much higher estimated prevalence of diarrhea compared to 2013 under all scenarios.
For Giardia, diarrhea estimates were most sensitive to model parameters representing viability, method recovery, and the concentration of Giardia observed in tubewell samples.

Cryptosporidium 7-day recall diarrhea period prevalence estimates were most sensitive to the rate parameter used in the dose-response model, the fraction of detected parasites assumed to be viable, and the method recovery of parasites from tubewell samples.
255.Rationale: Although concentration varies between years and is associated with differences in disease burden, the sensitivity analysis shows that for Cryptosporidium, concentration is not among the most sensitive parameters, and for Giardia it is one of several. The passage does not identify concentration as the primary driver of variation between years; other factors such as the fraction of parasites infectious-to-humans also substantially affect estimates.

255.Option: The morbidity ratio of symptomatic infections for Cryptosporidium and Giardia is the most sensitive parameter in the model, leading to the greatest variation in estimated disease burden.
Evidence: For both parasites, diarrhea estimates were least sensitive to ... the morbidity ratio ... as these parameters had sensitivity indices generally below 0.2.

Evidence: To account for the fraction of parasites in tubewell samples that are a species infectious-to-humans, we simulated three different scenarios...
assuming all parasites were shed from humans (HUM) increased diarrhea prevalence estimates by two-fold or more, compared to using fractions based on environmental parasite loading estimates (ENV)

The fraction of the observed all-cause diarrhea during each monsoon season that can be explained by the estimated tubewell drinking water protozoa infections under each human pathogenicity scenario (HUM, MST, ENV) is presented. The results indicate that as much as 65.8% or as little as 2.9% of the all-cause diarrhea burden in < 5 children can be attributed to waterborne infections from Cryptosporidium and Giardia in tubewell water.
255.Rationale: The passage demonstrates that the assumed fraction of parasites that are from humans and infectious-to-humans greatly influences risk and disease burden estimates, with different scenarios yielding widely varying attributable fractions, indicating this factor is key to variation in estimated disease burden.

In many cases, the disease begins to develop from acute necrotizing ulcerative gingivitis (ANUG) [2].

257.Option: The high level of non-synonymous polymorphisms in gp43 is due to sequencing errors and methodological artifacts, which artificially inflated the dN/dS ratio and led to false detection of positive selection.
Evidence: Therefore, mutational saturation is not responsible for the elevated dN/dS ratios observed in the virulence factors.

257.Option: The gp43 gene is under strong purifying selection, as indicated by the low levels of genetic variation and the absence of significant positive selection in the M-K test.
Evidence: Using the one-ratio model (M0), the average value of ω for the gp43 gene was 1.168 - significantly higher than for any of the housekeeping genes.
Selection models provided a significantly better fit to the data than the neutral models
257.Rationale: The passage shows that gp43 has an average ω (dN/dS) greater than 1, indicating positive selection, not purifying selection. While the M-K test for gp43 is not significant, other tests, such as codon-based likelihood analyses, provide strong evidence for positive selection. Therefore, the inference of purifying selection is incorrect based on the passage.

257.Option: The long time to the most recent common ancestor (TMRCA) of gp43 alleles indicates that the gene has undergone recent positive selection, leading to rapid fixation of beneficial mutations in the P. brasiliensis lineage.
Evidence: The results showed that the TRMCA for the gp43 alleles is longer than for any other gene in P. brasiliensis... This constitutes evidence for balancing selection [49],[50].
Conversely, the TRMCAs for cdc42, p27 and fks were significantly lower than the other genes as is expected if a gene is under positive directional selection.
257.Rationale: The passage directly associates long TMRCA with balancing selection, not recent positive selection. For genes under positive directional selection (like cdc42, p27), TMRCA is lower. Thus, the inference misinterprets the evidence and cannot be derived from the passage.

257.Option: The gp43 gene has evolved under positive directional selection, as evidenced by the significant results of the M-K test and the fixation of advantageous amino acid replacements in P. brasiliensis.
Evidence: For the gp43 case, the M-K test yielded no significant results between H. capsulatum and P. brasiliensis (Fischer's exact test, P = 0.40, Table 3).
The results showed that the TRMCA for the gp43 alleles is longer than for any other gene in P. brasiliensis... This constitutes evidence for balancing selection.
257.Rationale: The M-K test for gp43 is not significant, and the TMRCA evidence points to balancing selection, not positive directional selection. Positive directional selection is indicated for other genes (e.g., cdc42, p27) with significant M-K tests and lower TMRCA. Therefore, this inference is not supported by the passage.

257.Option: The persistence of non-synonymous intra- and trans-specific gp43 polymorphisms within and between lineages of the P. brasiliensis complex suggests they have been maintained by historical or contemporary selection.
Evidence: DNA sequences were obtained from 77 P. brasiliensis individuals that yielded twenty-six unique alleles... A total of 29 polymorphic sites and 33 mutations, including 8 singleton and 21 parsimony informative sites
Using the one-ratio model (M0), the average value of ω for the gp43 gene was 1.168 - significantly higher than for any of the housekeeping genes.
The results showed that the TRMCA for the gp43 alleles is longer than for any other gene in P. brasiliensis... This constitutes evidence for balancing selection [49],[50].
257.Rationale: The passage demonstrates that gp43 exhibits non-synonymous polymorphisms and shows evidence of selection (ω>1). The long TMRCA indicates balancing selection, which maintains genetic variation over time. This supports the inference that polymorphisms are maintained by selection, whether historical or contemporary.

258.Option: Heterotypic neutralizing antibodies, particularly against DENV-4, were more strongly associated with protection against DENV-2 infection than homotypic antibodies, suggesting that cross-protection may be more effective than serotype-specific immunity in some cases.
Evidence: For DENV-2 clusters, age-adjusted models were better using both homotypic DENV-2 reference NTs (AUC = 0.740 [95% CI 0.460, 0.960]) and heterotypic DENV-4 reference NTs (AUC = 0.880 [95% CI 0.680, 1.000]) than other heterotypic NTs (Table 3).
258.Rationale: The statistical findings show that for DENV-2 clusters, the model using heterotypic DENV-4 neutralizing antibody titers (NTs) had a higher area under the curve (AUC = 0.880) compared to homotypic DENV-2 NTs (AUC = 0.740), indicating a stronger association with protection against DENV-2 infection. This supports the inference that heterotypic antibodies, particularly against DENV-4, were more predictive of protection in this context, suggesting cross-protection may be notable.

258.Option: Pre-existing homotypic neutralizing antibody titers measured by PRNT were positively associated with protection against infection by DENV-1, -2, and -4, with the level of protection varying by serotype.
Evidence: When all serotypes were combined, PCR status was significantly associated with the log of homotypic NT both alone and adjusted for age (Table 2).
For DENV-1 clusters, age-adjusted models to predict PCR status were better (based on AIC and AUC) when using homotypic DENV-1 NTs (AUC = 0.833 [95% CI 0.590, 1.000]) than heterotypic NTs (Table 3).
For DENV-4 clusters, age-adjusted models were better using homotypic DENV-4 NTs (AUC = 0.889 [95% CI 0.667, 1.000]) than heterotypic NTs (Table 3).

For DENV-2 clusters, age-adjusted models were better using both homotypic DENV-2 reference NTs (AUC = 0.740 [95% CI 0.460, 0.960]) and heterotypic DENV-4 reference NTs (AUC = 0.880 [95% CI 0.680, 1.000]) than other heterotypic NTs (Table 3).
For all serotypes combined, the observed odds ratio of becoming infected (i.e., being PCR positive) if day 0 homotypic NT was ≥11 was 0.153 (95% CI 0.023, 0.700).
For DENV-1 clusters, the observed odds ratio if homotypic NT was ≥11 was 0 (95% CI 0.000, 1.554).
For DENV-4 clusters, the observed odds ratio if homotypic NT was ≥16 was 0 (95% CI 0.000, 0.842).
For DENV-2, the odds ratio if homotypic NT was ≥323 was 0 (95% CI 0.000, 1.279).
258.Rationale: The passage demonstrates that higher homotypic neutralizing antibody titers (NTs) are associated with lower odds of infection for DENV-1, DENV-2, and DENV-4, as shown by significant logistic regression models and odds ratios. The cutoff titers for protection vary by serotype (e.g., 11 for DENV-1, 16 for DENV-4, 323 for DENV-2), indicating that the level of protection required differs across serotypes.

258.Option: The protective effect of neutralizing antibodies is consistent across all dengue serotypes, with similar titers required for protection regardless of the infecting serotype.
Evidence: Homotypic NT cutoff values were 11, 11, 16 and 323 for all serotypes combined, DENV-1, DENV-4, and DENV-2, respectively.
For DENV-1 clusters, the observed odds ratio if homotypic NT was ≥11 was 0 (95% CI 0.000, 1.554).
For DENV-4 clusters, the observed odds ratio if homotypic NT was ≥16 was 0 (95% CI 0.000, 0.842).
For DENV-2, the odds ratio if homotypic NT was ≥323 was 0 (95% CI 0.000, 1.279).
258.Rationale: The evidence shows that the homotypic neutralizing antibody titers associated with protection differ substantially by serotype: DENV-1 and DENV-4 have low cutoffs (11 and 16), while DENV-2 has a much higher cutoff (323). This indicates that the protective effect is not consistent across serotypes, and the titers required for protection are not similar, contradicting the option.

258.Option: The lack of clinical efficacy against DENV-2 in the recent dengue vaccine trial in Thailand may have partly been due to insufficiently high DENV-2 NTs, suggesting that vaccine-induced antibody levels should be optimized to match natural infection thresholds.
Evidence: Recently, the first human efficacy trial of a dengue vaccine candidate was completed in Thailand showing good neutralizing antibody response to all four DENV serotypes after vaccination, but no clinical efficacy against DENV-2 infection.
This was done in order to evaluate possible differences in PRNT titers against different DENV-2 strains given the lack of vaccine efficacy against DENV-2 reported in a recent dengue vaccine trial.
For DENV-2, the odds ratio if homotypic NT was ≥323 was 0 (95% CI 0.000, 1.279).
258.Rationale: The passage links the vaccine trial's lack of efficacy against DENV-2 to the study's investigation of DENV-2 neutralizing antibody titers. The findings show that a high homotypic NT (323) is associated with protection against DENV-2 infection. From this, it can be inferred that if vaccine-induced NTs were below this threshold, it might explain the lack of efficacy, suggesting that optimizing vaccine-induced antibody levels to reach natural infection thresholds could improve efficacy.

258.Option: The study design using geographic clusters with matched exposed but uninfected individuals provides definitive evidence that neutralizing antibodies are the primary mechanism of protection against dengue virus infection.
Evidence: The relevance of DENV neutralizing antibodies for protection or modulation of DENV infection, therefore, remains unclear.
Up to now, no epidemiological study in humans has been able to demonstrate an association between pre-existing NTs and protection from infection.
We sought to test the hypothesis that neutralizing antibody titers immediately before exposure was associated with the probability of infection.
258.Rationale: The passage explicitly states that the relevance of neutralizing antibodies is unclear and that no previous study had shown an association. This study demonstrates an association between pre-existing NTs and infection status, but it does not establish definitive evidence or that NTs are the primary mechanism of protection. The study design shows correlation, not causation, and the passage does not claim primacy or definitive proof; it only reports an association.

259.Option: Sub-microscopic P. vivax infections are primarily detected in symptomatic patients due to the higher parasite density associated with clinical illness, as shown by the higher PCR prevalence in fever patient surveys.
Evidence: This suggests that amongst symptomatic patient populations SM P. vivax and P. falciparum are not prevalent.
between 88.6 and 100.0% (average 97.1%) of individuals with SM P. vivax were asymptomatic.
259.Rationale: The passage indicates that SM P. vivax infections are predominantly asymptomatic, with an average of 97.1% of individuals showing no symptoms. Furthermore, in surveys of fever patients, PCR did not detect significantly more infections than LM, suggesting that SM infections are not prevalent in symptomatic populations. Therefore, the claim that SM infections are primarily detected in symptomatic patients is contradicted by the evidence.

259.Option: The high proportion of asymptomatic sub-microscopic P. vivax infections in the community is primarily due to recent antimalarial drug use, which rapidly reduces parasite density and prevents symptom onset.
Evidence: Only one survey reported that recent antimalarial treatment (<4 weeks prior to survey) was associated with a significant increase in risk of having SM P. vivax infection [33].
259.Rationale: While one survey found an association between recent antimalarial drug use and SM infections, it is not presented as the primary cause. The passage does not attribute the high proportion of asymptomatic SM infections primarily to drug use; instead, factors such as host immunity and biological features of P. vivax are discussed. Thus, this inference cannot be derived from the passage.

259.Option: The use of whole blood for DNA extraction in PCR assays significantly increases the risk of false-positive results in detecting sub-microscopic P. vivax infections, which undermines the reliability of PCR-based surveillance.
Evidence: The average prevalence of SM P. vivax in community surveys determined by PCR using whole blood was significantly higher than that using blood from filter papers (Mann Whitney test, P = 0.004).
259.Rationale: The passage shows that using whole blood for DNA extraction leads to higher detection of SM P. vivax infections compared to filter paper, likely due to increased sensitivity or DNA yield. However, there is no mention of false-positive results or that it undermines reliability; the difference is attributed to methodological factors, not inaccuracy. Therefore, this inference is not supported.

259.Option: The prevalence of sub-microscopic P. vivax infections is significantly higher in areas with high transmission intensity compared to low transmission intensity, as indicated by the higher number of PCR-positive cases in these regions.
Evidence: A negative relationship between LM P. vivax prevalence and proportion of SM P. vivax infections was identified
PCR prevalence of P. vivax was significantly higher than that of LM
259.Rationale: The passage demonstrates a negative relationship between LM prevalence (a proxy for transmission intensity) and the proportion of SM infections, meaning that in areas with lower transmission intensity, the proportion of SM infections is higher. However, it does not directly compare the absolute prevalence of SM infections across transmission intensities. While PCR prevalence is higher in high transmission areas, this includes both microscopic and SM infections, and the proportion of SM is lower. Thus, the inference that SM prevalence is significantly higher in high transmission areas is not derivable from the evidence.

259.Option: The relative proportion of sub-microscopic P. vivax infections is significantly higher than that of the sympatric P. falciparum in the same study settings, and this proportion increases in areas with lower transmission intensity.
Evidence: the average relative proportion of SM was significantly higher in P. vivax infections compared to P. falciparum in the same study (Wilcoxon matched-pairs signed rank test, Median difference  = 5.2%, P = 0.045, Fig. 5B).
A negative relationship between LM P. vivax prevalence and proportion of SM P. vivax infections was identified
259.Rationale: The passage explicitly states that the relative proportion of SM infections is significantly higher for P. vivax than for P. falciparum in the same settings. Additionally, the negative relationship between LM prevalence and proportion of SM infections indicates that as LM prevalence decreases (i.e., in areas with lower transmission intensity), the proportion of SM P. vivax infections increases. This directly supports both parts of the inference.

260.Option: The high level of awareness about NTDs among medical and nursing students in Egypt is capable of achieving significant and long-lasting results in controlling these diseases.
Evidence: Only one fourth (26%) of the students knew the meaning of NTDs
Low percentage of the studied medical and nursing students (ranged from 4.3 to 23.9%) correctly identified the names of individual NTDs
Very low percentage of the students (5%) knew specific activities for NTDs prevention and control in Egypt
260.Rationale: The passage demonstrates low awareness levels among students, not a high level. Only 26% knew the meaning of NTDs, and identification of specific diseases was poor. Therefore, the claim of high awareness is contradicted by the data, and no evidence supports that current student awareness is capable of achieving significant control results.

260.Option: The lack of explicit mention of the term 'neglected tropical diseases' (NTDs) in the epidemiology course materials at Cairo University is associated with a significant knowledge gap among medical and nursing students regarding NTDs and their control activities.
Evidence: Neither the term 'neglected tropical disease' nor the abbreviation 'NTDs' was mentioned explicitly in the course materials or specifications.
Only one fourth (26%) of the students knew the meaning of NTDs
Low percentage of the studied medical and nursing students (ranged from 4.3 to 23.9%) correctly identified the names of individual NTDs
Very low percentage of the students (5%) knew specific activities for NTDs prevention and control in Egypt
The epidemiology course was mentioned as a single source of knowledge by 8 students (4.3%), while it was mentioned with other sources by 6 students (3.3%)
260.Rationale: The study finds that the epidemiology course does not explicitly introduce the concept of NTDs as a group, and students show poor knowledge about NTDs meaning, specific diseases, and control activities. While causation cannot be proven, the cross-sectional data show an association: the absence of the term in the curriculum coincides with low awareness and minimal reporting of the course as a knowledge source. This is a reasonable epidemiological inference from the observed relationship.

260.Option: The lack of NTDs in the curriculum is not a significant factor in the low knowledge levels among students, as most students learned about NTDs through social media and mass media.
Evidence: Only one fourth (26%) of the students knew the meaning of NTDs; the main source of their knowledge is social media, followed by epidemiology course and mass media (TV/Radio)
Neither the term 'neglected tropical disease' nor the abbreviation 'NTDs' was mentioned explicitly in the course materials or specifications.
260.Rationale: The passage does not provide evidence to assess the significance of curricular lack as a factor. While social media and mass media are reported as main sources, this does not imply that curriculum is not a significant factor; it may indicate that because the curriculum does not cover NTDs adequately, students turn to other sources. The study does not analyze factors contributing to low knowledge, so this inference cannot be drawn.

260.Option: The inclusion of NTDs in the epidemiology course curriculum would likely lead to a significant increase in student willingness to participate in NTDs control activities.
Evidence: more than one third of them (34%) expressed their willingness to participate in these control activities
No statistically significant difference (P value = 0.061) was found between medical and nursing students regarding their willingness to participate in the control activities for NTDs
260.Rationale: The study does not test the effect of curriculum inclusion on willingness. While 34% expressed willingness, there is no evidence linking curriculum content to willingness. The lack of significant difference between medical and nursing students (who had similar knowledge levels) does not support a relationship between knowledge and willingness. Therefore, this inference is speculative and not supported by the findings.

260.Option: The poor public awareness of NTDs in Egypt is primarily due to insufficient media coverage of control programs, despite adequate knowledge among healthcare students.
Evidence: Only one fourth (26%) of the students knew the meaning of NTDs
nearly half of the students agreed on the poor level of awareness regarding NTDs in Egypt
260.Rationale: The passage indicates that healthcare students themselves have low knowledge (not adequate), and while media is a source, there is no evidence that insufficient media coverage is the primary cause of poor public awareness. The study does not investigate causes of public awareness, so this inference cannot be derived.

261.Option: The method is capable of detecting the effect of benznidazole on T. cruzi epimastigotes at concentrations as low as 0.0195 μg/mL, indicating high sensitivity to drug action.
Evidence: the activity of the Biospeckle pattern of 4x104 parasites per well, at times of 1, 61, 128 and 204 minutes with six concentrations of benznidazol ranging from 0.0195 to 20μg/mL
benznidazole produces an instantaneous effect at 1min on T. cruzi that occurs at all the tested concentrations
In the present work, the concentration of benznidazole is used in a range of 0.0195 to 20μg/mL (from 0.075 to 76.8μM) and the activity is detected in a minimum time of 1 minute.
261.Rationale: The passage explicitly states that the method tested benznidazole concentrations down to 0.0195 μg/mL and detected an instantaneous effect at 1 minute for all tested concentrations, including the lowest. This directly supports the inference that the method is sensitive enough to detect drug effects at low concentrations.

261.Option: The Biospeckle pattern is sensitive to changes in parasite motility and aggregation, which may be influenced by drug-induced taxis, suggesting that the method detects early behavioral responses to benznidazole.
Evidence: the activity of the Biospeckle pattern is taken as a measure of the activity of the parasites
261.Rationale: While the passage indicates that the Biospeckle pattern reflects parasite activity, it does not specify that this activity is related to motility, aggregation, or drug-induced taxis. The inference about early behavioral responses is speculative and not directly supported by evidence in the passage; the method detects changes in activity broadly, but the specific mechanisms are not discussed.

261.Option: The activity of the Biospeckle pattern is proportional to the quantity of parasites present in the assay well and remains stable despite desiccation up to around 200 minutes.
Evidence: the three approaches are able to detect an increase in the activity of the Biospeckle pattern as a function of parasite quantity. Moreover, the plots are nearly linear
the Biospeckle pattern is proportional to the quantity of parasites in the test wells within the evaluated range
Fig 4 shows that the number of parasites of both experiments can fit a linear regression.
the results indicate that although there is a marked desiccation effect, the Biospeckle pattern remains stable up to around 200 minutes
261.Rationale: The passage provides direct evidence that the Biospeckle pattern increases linearly with parasite number, indicating proportionality, and that the pattern remains stable over time despite desiccation, up to approximately 200 minutes. Both aspects are explicitly stated.

261.Option: The Biospeckle pattern remains stable over time because the VDRL plate's flat-bottomed design minimizes optical distortion and prevents evaporation-induced signal drift.
Evidence: The design and dimensions of the VDRL flat test wells are adapted to optical microscopes and magnifiers
the desiccation of the liquid in the air exposed VDRL wells ... there is a marked desiccation effect
261.Rationale: The passage does not state that the flat-bottomed design minimizes optical distortion or prevents evaporation-induced drift. While the VDRL plate is adapted for optical use, the stability of the Biospeckle pattern is noted despite desiccation, not because evaporation is prevented. In fact, evaporation occurs, so the design does not prevent it; the pattern remains stable for other reasons (e.g., detection of total parasites). The causal claim is not supported.

262.Option: Tungiasis is an important health problem in Vihiga County occasioned by the low economic status of the people affected, and factors that point to poverty contribute to the occurrence of tungiasis in Vihiga County.
Evidence: Tungiasis also referred to as jigger infestation is a neglected tropical disease due to the fact that it typically occurs in populations living in poverty, with poor housing, inadequate sanitation and in close contact with domestic animals akin to other neglected diseases.
The poverty levels are high (41%) and the GDP per capita income was reported as US $ 1,103 in 2013.
In the multivariate analysis, the occurrence of tungiasis was significantly associated with variables that indicated low economic status (like monthly income <1000 adjusted OR 27.85 (4.13–187.59)
Others retained in the model though not significant were earthen floor, toilet availability, occasional or not using closed footwear and common resting place inside the house.
262.Rationale: The passage explicitly describes tungiasis as a disease of poverty and provides evidence from the study area (high poverty levels) and multivariate analysis showing low income as a significant risk factor with a high odds ratio. Factors like earthen floors and lack of sanitation, which are indicators of poverty, are also associated. This directly supports the inference that low economic status and poverty-related conditions contribute to the occurrence of tungiasis in Vihiga County.

262.Option: The high prevalence of tungiasis among children aged 5–14 years is primarily due to their frequent exposure to infected domestic animals, especially rats, in shared household spaces.
Evidence: age group ≤14, school going, self-treatment, occasional or not using closed footwear were shown to be significantly associated with tungiasis
having rats in the compound

In the multivariate analysis, the occurrence of tungiasis was significantly associated with variables that indicated low economic status (like monthly income <1000 adjusted OR 27.85 (4.13–187.59) and age (≤14 years) of participant adjusted OR 27.414 (10.02–74.99)
262.Rationale: While the passage shows that age ≤14 is significantly associated with tungiasis and that having rats in the compound is a factor in bivariate analysis, it does not establish that exposure to rats is the primary reason for high prevalence in children. The multivariate analysis highlights low income and age as significant, but rats are not mentioned in the multivariate results. The option claims a primary causal link that cannot be derived from the evidence provided; other factors like poverty and behavioral aspects may be more salient.

262.Option: The lack of knowledge about tungiasis transmission among 45% of participants suggests that community education campaigns should be prioritized to eliminate stigma and improve treatment adherence.
Evidence: 45% of the participants interviewed had no knowledge on its transmission.
262.Rationale: The passage reports the percentage of participants lacking knowledge about transmission, but it does not infer or suggest any public health interventions such as prioritizing education campaigns. The Passage Body (which excludes the Discussion) presents this as a finding without drawing conclusions about implications for stigma or treatment adherence. This option requires external reasoning about public health actions not derived from the epidemiological evidence in the Passage Body.

262.Option: The absence of spatial autocorrelation in tungiasis cases suggests that environmental factors such as soil type and elevation are the primary drivers of disease transmission in the study area.
Evidence: The semivariogram revealed no spatial autocorrelation which could indicate that tungiasis prevalence in the study area might be random.
The soil type in the three sub locations did not vary, it was mainly observed to be sandy clay.
Visual investigation of the distribution of cases did not reveal any obvious spatial pattern in terms of the changes in elevation.
262.Rationale: The passage indicates no spatial autocorrelation, suggesting a random distribution of cases, and notes that soil type was consistent and elevation showed no pattern. This contradicts the claim that environmental factors like soil and elevation are primary drivers; instead, it implies that non-spatial factors such as socio-economic conditions (e.g., poverty and age) are more influential, as supported by the multivariate analysis.

262.Option: The use of GIS and spatial interpolation techniques such as inverse distance weighting (IDW) allows for accurate prediction of tungiasis hotspots in small-scale endemic areas, enabling targeted public health interventions.
Evidence: The tungiasis case concentration map was produced by interpolating the number of cases recorded per site using the inverse distance weighting (IDW) algorithm tool found in ArcGIS Spatial Analyst tool
The semivariogram revealed no spatial autocorrelation which could indicate that tungiasis prevalence in the study area might be random.
262.Rationale: While the passage describes the use of GIS and IDW for mapping, it also reports no spatial autocorrelation, indicating that cases are randomly distributed and thus interpolation may not accurately predict hotspots. The passage does not conclude that accurate prediction is possible; it merely presents the methodological approach without validating its predictive accuracy. The inference that IDW enables accurate hotspot prediction is not supported by the evidence and is contradicted by the randomness suggested by spatial analysis.

263.Option: The increasing dengue incidence from 1999 to 2008, coupled with a stable median age of infection, suggests that the force of infection has declined, leading to fewer infections in children and a higher proportion of symptomatic cases in adults.
Evidence: In this analysis of years 1999-2008, the age adjusted dengue incidence increased 1.38 fold annually (table 1).

Although the average age of notified dengue cases increased over the study period, from a median (mean) age of 20 (22.4) years in 1998 to 24 (26.8) years in 2009, this was not statistically significant after adjusting for changes in the population age structure (figure 2B).
263.Rationale: The passage shows increasing incidence from 1999 to 2008, but a decline in force of infection would typically cause the median age of infection to increase, not remain stable. The stable median age after adjustment does not support a decline in force of infection. Moreover, the passage does not provide evidence that fewer infections in children or a higher proportion of symptomatic cases in adults are linked to a declining force of infection; the high proportion of adult cases was already present.

263.Option: Despite a significant increase in age-adjusted dengue incidence in Hanoi from 1999 to 2008, the median age of notified cases did not decrease, suggesting that the force of infection may not have increased and that clinical case reporting patterns may be influenced by factors such as adult susceptibility to symptomatic disease.
Evidence: In this analysis of years 1999-2008, the age adjusted dengue incidence increased 1.38 fold annually (table 1).

Although the average age of notified dengue cases increased over the study period, from a median (mean) age of 20 (22.4) years in 1998 to 24 (26.8) years in 2009, this was not statistically significant after adjusting for changes in the population age structure (figure 2B).
The majority of notified cases (85%) occurred in individuals aged over 15 years (figure 2A).
263.Rationale: The evidence shows a significant increase in age-adjusted incidence from 1999 to 2008, but no significant change in the median age after adjustment. In dengue epidemiology, an increase in force of infection (transmission intensity) typically shifts the age distribution of cases toward younger ages. The absence of such a shift suggests that the force of infection may not have increased substantially. The high and stable proportion of adult cases indicates that clinical reporting may be biased toward adults, possibly due to higher susceptibility to symptomatic disease or healthcare-seeking behavior in this age group, which is consistent with the patterns observed in a low transmission setting like Hanoi.

263.Option: The increase in dengue incidence from 1999 to 2008 is likely due to improved case detection and reporting, as evidenced by the rising proportion of IgM-positive cases over time.
Evidence: The overall, proportion of ELISA tests that were anti-dengue virus specific IgM positive was 23.7%, ranging from 13.6% in 2006 to 49.8% in 2003. The lowest annual test positivity rates were observed in 2006 (13.6%), 2007 (17.6%), and 2008 (16.1%).
The proportion of cases with an available ELISA result varied from 68.2% in 2002 to 4.5% in 2009.
263.Rationale: The passage does not show a rising trend in IgM positivity over time; instead, positivity rates fluctuated and were lowest in the later years (2006-2008). The proportion of cases with ELISA results also decreased dramatically, suggesting that laboratory confirmation was not consistently applied. Therefore, the evidence does not support improved case detection or reporting as the cause of the incidence increase.

263.Option: The high average age of notified dengue cases in Hanoi reflects a relatively recent introduction of dengue into the population, resulting in a lack of multitypic immunity among adults and a corresponding increase in symptomatic disease among older individuals.
Evidence: This work was conceived as the first step in studying the process of dengue emergence in Hanoi and as a framework for developing further studies of the determinants of dengue infection and disease risk in a low transmission setting.
Increasing numbers of notified dengue cases have led to concerns that dengue is ‘emerging’ in Hanoi, and in 2009 Hanoi experienced its largest ever-recorded outbreak of dengue.
263.Rationale: While the passage indicates that dengue may be emerging in Hanoi, it does not provide evidence that the high average age of cases is due to recent introduction. The high age could be explained by low transmission intensity (as noted in the passage) rather than recent introduction. Moreover, the study period (1998-2009) shows established annual outbreaks, suggesting dengue was already present before 1998. Thus, the inference about recent introduction is not directly derivable from the trends in incidence and age distribution between 1999 and 2008.

264.Option: We found metacercariae of Amphimerus sp. in all fish species recognized by local people as edible, belonging to three different families (i.e., Characidae, Cichlidae, and Lebiasinidae, Fig 3 and Table 2). Considering the high number of fish species involved in the life cycles of other opistorchiid trematodes, such as C. sinensis and O. viverrini (32 fish species [46] and 11 fish species, respectively [47]), we surmise that our number is an underestimation of the total number of fish species involved in the life cycle of Amphimerus sp.; regardless, a limited number of second intermediate hosts has been reported for the genus elsewhere [9,25].
Evidence: Through artificial digestion experiments, we found numerous metacercaria in all of the fish species

We collected 649 fish belonging to four species: Rhoadsia altipinna (n = 504, 77.66%, family = Characidae), Bryconamericus bucay (n = 80, 12.33%, family = Characidae), Andinoacora rivulatus (n = 58, 8.93%, family = Cichlidae), and Piabucina aureoguttata (n = 7, 1.08%, family = Lebiasinidae)
264.Rationale: The Passage Body confirms that metacercariae were found in all fish species collected, which are from three families, and these fish are recognized as edible by locals. However, the inference that the number of fish species involved is an underestimation based on comparisons with other opisthorchiid trematodes (e.g., C. sinensis and O. viverrini) is not derived from the Passage Body. The Passage Body does not provide data or references on the number of fish species for those trematodes in this context, and such speculation is epidemiological reasoning that extends beyond the evidence presented. Therefore, the full option cannot be logically derived solely from the Passage Body.

264.Option: The high prevalence of 35.8% in the present study corresponds to a population of mestizo inhabitants [29] who instead of being connected by rivers, live in a human-populated, dry forest landscape connected by roads, and watered by small streams feeding into the Pedro Pablo Gómez and Grande Rivers (Figs 1 and 5); in this area, use of fish as a daily food source is secondary.
Evidence: all connected by different branches and streams of the Pedro Pablo Gómez and Grande Rivers
As a rural population, they depend on their own vegetable crops, livestock, hunting, and fishing as food sources
264.Rationale: The Passage Body describes the study area as connected by rivers and streams, not primarily by roads. It also lists fishing as one of several food sources without indicating that it is secondary. The option's claims about road connectivity and secondary fish consumption are not supported by the Passage Body. Additionally, the question focuses on prevalence based on diagnostic methods and study design, not on population characteristics or landscape. Thus, this option cannot be derived from the Passage Body through epidemiological reasoning.

264.Option: The high prevalence of small trematode eggs in the human population of the three communities reported here may still be an underestimate considering the low sensitivity of small trematode egg detection using the formalin-ether concentration technique and one-time fecal sample examination.
Evidence: One fecal sample was collected from each local resident above one year old without other restriction criteria.
For coproparasitological diagnosis, we used the formalin-ether concentration method (Ritchie’s technique)

Molecular and immunological diagnostic tools have been developed to discriminate between opisthorchiid and heterophyid eggs in Asia where their burden has been studied extensively (e.g., Thailand) [10,20]. In Ecuador, Amphimerus sp. diagnosis has been explored using multiple coproparasitological methods, which to today remain the gold standard for small trematode egg identification [15]. Recently, immunological and molecular methods based on enzyme-linked immunosorbent assay (ELISA) and loop-mediated isothermal amplification (LAMP) have been developed, which could improve case diagnosis and detection [21–23].
264.Rationale: The Passage Body reports a prevalence of 35.8% based on one-time fecal sample examination using the formalin-ether concentration method. Epidemiological principles suggest that single-sample examinations and concentration methods may have limited sensitivity for parasitic infections due to intermittent egg shedding. The Passage Body further supports this by mentioning that molecular and immunological tools have been developed to improve diagnosis, implying that current methods might be less sensitive. Therefore, it is logically inferable that the reported prevalence may be an underestimate.

265.Option: The high ICs to sulfamethoxazole/trimethoprim observed in most isolates under laboratory conditions suggest that current first-line treatments are ineffective in clinical settings.
Evidence: To our surprise, most isolates showed high ICs to sulfamethoxazole/trimethoprim (Fig 7).
The first-line treatment of actinomycetoma is a combination of sulfamethoxazole/trimethoprim and amoxicillin/clavulanic acid [36].
265.Rationale: While the Passage Body reports high in vitro inhibitory concentrations (ICs) to first-line antibiotics, it does not provide evidence linking these laboratory findings to clinical treatment outcomes. Epidemiological inference regarding treatment effectiveness requires data on patient responses or clinical failure, which are not presented.
