The remaining 21 RBT-positive sera had a titre 1:2 (i.e., only agglutinated with the plain serum) and of these only two had a 1:640 titre and 12 had a 1:320 titre with BrucellaCapt.
330.Rationale: The passage shows that RBT-positive sera were subjected to serial dilution and complementary BrucellaCapt testing, indicating that standard RBT alone is not considered sufficient for diagnosis. The variability in RBT titers and the need for further testing to confirm cases, especially with low titers like 1:2, suggest that additional confirmatory testing is necessary. No conclusion is drawn about the sufficiency of RBT with titer ≥1:4; instead, the study uses a combination of tests for evaluation.

330.Option: The BrucellaCapt test is superior to the standard RBT for diagnosing brucellosis in Uganda because it detects both acute and long evolution cases and has higher sensitivity.
Evidence: the acid pH agglutination tests, namely the RBT and BrucellaCapt, which detect both acute and long evolution cases
both of which have been validated and are valuable in the detection of contacts and short and long evolution cases of human brucellosis.

Among the ruminant slaughterhouse workers, the sero-prevalence according to results of the standard RBT protocol was 9.0% (31/344) (95% CI: 6.3–12.7). Considering the BrucellaCapt test and a titre ≥ 320 the seroprevalence was 7.3% (25/344) (95% CI: 4.8–10.7)
330.Rationale: The passage presents RBT and BrucellaCapt as complementary tests that both detect acute and long evolution cases, with no statement that BrucellaCapt is superior. The seroprevalence figures differ, but no interpretation is provided regarding relative sensitivity or superiority. The passage emphasizes the utility of both tests in combination, and for point-of-care, RBT is highlighted as well-suited for resource-limited settings. Therefore, a conclusion that BrucellaCapt is superior cannot be derived from the Passage Body.

331.Option: The identification of compounds that inhibit both TGR and Prx2 in the dual-enzyme assay suggests that the most effective therapeutic strategy for schistosomiasis may involve dual-target inhibition of the parasite's redox cascade.
Evidence: When tested against Prx2 in a GR-coupled hydrogen peroxide reduction assay none of the selected compounds showed activity up to the 50 µM top concentration tested (and by extension, none were active against GR, an enzyme related to TGR). Prx2 was therefore ruled out as the target of any of the actives identified in the screen.
331.Rationale: Target deconvolution experiments explicitly showed that none of the active compounds inhibited Prx2; they only inhibited TGR. Therefore, the compounds do not inhibit both enzymes, and the inference about dual-target inhibition as the most effective strategy is not supported by the evidence.

331.Option: The successful identification of potent inhibitors of TGR, combined with the lack of inhibition of human GR, suggests that these compounds may be used as broad-spectrum antiparasitic agents effective against multiple neglected tropical diseases.
Evidence: In contrast, humans possess two distinct enzymes, glutathione reductase (GR) and thioredoxin reductase (TrxR), which specifically recognize GSH and Trx as substrates, respectively.

When tested against Prx2 in a GR-coupled hydrogen peroxide reduction assay none of the selected compounds showed activity up to the 50 µM top concentration tested (and by extension, none were active against GR, an enzyme related to TGR).
331.Rationale: While the passage notes that human GR and TrxR are distinct from TGR, it does not report testing the identified compounds against human GR. The lack of inhibition mentioned refers to a yeast GR used in the assay, not human GR. Moreover, the passage focuses solely on schistosomiasis and S. mansoni; there is no evidence that these compounds are effective against other neglected tropical diseases, so the inference about broad-spectrum activity is not derivable.

331.Option: The identification of several novel structural series of TGR inhibitors, several of which are highly potent, provides both mechanistic tools for probing the redox balance in S. mansoni and starting points for developing medicinal leads for much-needed new treatments for schistosomiasis.
Evidence: preliminary reports of praziquantel-resistant cases, and the generation of praziquantel-resistant parasites in the laboratory [9]–[11] highlight the need for new drugs to treat the disease.
Such improved understanding of the organisms responsible for neglected tropical diseases (NTDs) presents opportunities for new drug development.

The MLI provides academic investigators with the pharma-scale infrastructure and technologies necessary to discover both chemical probes of physiology, and starting points for development of novel therapeutics for the rare and neglected diseases that are of less interest to the pharmaceutical sector.
Analysis of the qHTS results revealed 39 actives characterized by full concentration-response curves and IC50 values of better than 10 µM.

oxadiazole 2-oxides (5 actives out of 29 analogues in collection, IC50 potency range between 8 µM and inactive), phosphinic amides (two compounds in the collection, one inactive and one active at 37 nM), phosphoramidite (singleton active, IC50 of 560 nM), and isoxazolone (singleton active, IC50 of 530 nM).

Gratifyingly, the previously-untested analogues of the phosphinic amides (compounds 1–4), phosphoramidite (13–16), and isoxazolone (10–12) actives all showed activity with various degrees of potency, supporting and expanding the qHTS findings.

The majority of active compounds demonstrated approximately the same, and in some instances improved (most notably with 7 and 8), potency against the isolated TGR as they did against the dual-enzyme system. These results not only confirm the initial findings from the screen, but also further support the hypothesis of TGR being the sole target of these actives.
331.Rationale: The passage clearly states the need for new drugs due to emerging resistance, describes the discovery of novel TGR inhibitor series with potent IC50 values, and explicitly links the project to the goal of developing chemical probes and therapeutic leads. Therefore, this inference is directly supported by the evidence.

332.Option: The expansion of CD4+CD25+FOXP3+ T cells in hookworm-infected individuals is primarily driven by the direct induction of FOXP3 expression in naïve T cells by excretory-secretory products of nematodes.
Evidence: The parasite may promote its survival by excreting/secreting a panel of molecular immunosuppressive agents and, possibly, by stimulating the appearance of regulatory T-cell populations.
Taken together, our results suggest that Treg cells may play an important role in hookworm-induced immunosuppression, contributing to the longevity of hookworm survival in infected people.
332.Rationale: The passage suggests that excretory-secretory products may stimulate Treg populations, but it does not provide evidence that this expansion is 'primarily driven' by direct induction of FOXP3 in naïve T cells. The quoted statements are speculative or describe a correlation, not a mechanism. No experiments in the passage test direct induction of FOXP3 by ES products on naïve T cells.

332.Option: The absence of changes in T cell numbers or Treg counts in human primo-infection with N. americanus suggests that Treg-mediated immunosuppression is not a primary mechanism of hookworm survival in early infection.
Evidence: Ten volunteers between the ages of 18 and 76 were recruited over the course of two months (Table 1). These volunteers reside in areas of moderate N. americanus transmission and presented with low to moderate (up to 872 epg) intensity of Necator infection.
Human hookworm infection is a longstanding, chronic infection with complex life cycle stages and host-parasite interactions.
332.Rationale: The study population consists of individuals with chronic hookworm infection, not primo-infection (early infection). The passage does not investigate primo-infection, so no inferences about Treg dynamics in early infection can be drawn. In fact, the passage shows significant increases in Treg counts in infected individuals compared to non-infected, contradicting the 'absence of changes' claim for the studied population.

332.Option: The reduction in CD4+CD25+FOXP3+IL-17+ cells after hookworm antigen stimulation indicates that IL-17 production is directly suppressed by parasite antigens, leading to a permanent shift toward a regulatory phenotype.
Evidence: Whole blood was stimulated in vitro with ES and crude antigens (5 µg/well) in RPMI 1640 media supplemented with 1.6% L-glutamine (Sigma, USA), 3% antibiotic-antimycotic (Invitrogen, USA), 5% of heat inactivated AB+ human serum (Sigma, USA), for 24 hours at 37°C with 5% CO2.

When crude antigen was added to the in vitro cultures, the percentage and the absolute counts of CD4+CD25+FOXP3+ regulatory T cells co-expressing IL-17 was significantly reduced (p<0.0030) (Figure 3).
332.Rationale: While the passage shows a reduction in IL-17+ Tregs after in vitro antigen stimulation, it does not demonstrate that this suppression is 'direct' (as opposed to indirect) or that it leads to a 'permanent shift.' The experiment was short-term (24 hours), and no follow-up data on long-term effects are provided. The passage also does not show a shift toward a regulatory phenotype beyond this specific subset.

332.Option: Hookworm-induced Treg activity may be able to control and divert selective proliferative and cytokine responses to numerous disorders, such as intestinal inflammation, airways inflammation/hyper-reactivity, diabetes, and multiple sclerosis.
Evidence: The discovery of regulatory T lymphocytes (Treg) that are actively involved in maintaining immune tolerance has recently led to new insights into mechanisms of tolerance breakdown and/or immunoregulation in human diseases, including those resulting from allergic, autoimmune, or infectious causes.
Taken together, our results suggest that Treg cells may play an important role in hookworm-induced immunosuppression, contributing to the longevity of hookworm survival in infected people.
332.Rationale: The passage mentions that Tregs are involved in immune tolerance in various diseases, but it does not provide any evidence that hookworm-induced Tregs can control or divert responses to specific disorders like diabetes or multiple sclerosis. The study only examines hookworm-specific immune responses and does not test cross-reactivity or effects on other diseases. The inference is speculative and not directly derivable from the data presented.

332.Option: The increased expression of GITR and CTLA-4 on Treg cells in hookworm-infected individuals is sufficient to fully account for the observed suppression of antigen-specific proliferation, independent of cytokine production.
Evidence: Infection of N. americanus significantly increased the proportion of cells expressing CTLA-4 (p=0.0002) and GITR (p<0.0001).

The depletion of Tregs elicited the increase of IL-2 and lower levels of IL-10 in supernatants of dPBMCs with or without antigenic stimulation although statistical significance was not achieved (Supplementary Figure S4).

However, in vitro cultures, where Tregs cells were depleted (dPBMC), showed a significant increase on the CD4+ cell proliferative response induced by crude (p=0.0039) and ES (p=0.0012) hookworm antigenic stimulation (Figure 4A).
332.Rationale: While the passage shows increased expression of GITR and CTLA-4 on Tregs and that Treg depletion augments proliferation, it does not demonstrate that these molecules are 'sufficient' to account for suppression or that their effect is independent of cytokines. The passage also notes changes in cytokines (IL-2, IL-10) upon Treg depletion, suggesting cytokine involvement. No mechanistic experiments dissecting the roles of GITR, CTLA-4, and cytokines are presented, so the claim is overreaching.

333.Option: In endemic areas of intestinal schistosomiasis with low-intensity infections, the actual prevalence can be underestimated by up to 4.7 times when measured by the recommended standard procedure.
Evidence: In this new epidemiological scenario, most of the infected individuals in endemic areas harbor low parasite loads and are very unlikely to be detected with the commonly used parasitological methods.
the real prevalence in an endemic setting may be significantly underestimated

the prevalence found in each age group, considering the combination of all parasitological exams (reference standard), was 1.7 to 4.7 times higher than the prevalence obtained with the recommended two KK slides from one fecal sample (Fig 4).
333.Rationale: The passage directly addresses a community with long-term control interventions and low parasite burden, noting that standard parasitological methods like the Kato-Katz technique have reduced sensitivity in such settings. Epidemiological data from the study show that the reference standard, which combines multiple sensitive methods, yields prevalence estimates that are 1.7 to 4.7 times higher than those from the standard Kato-Katz method, depending on age group. This supports the inference that the standard procedure can underestimate true prevalence by up to 4.7 times in areas with low-intensity infections.

334.Option: The hyaluronidase from Loxosceles venom is essential for the activation of phospholipase-D, thereby enabling the toxin to exert its dermonecrotic effects.
Evidence: Dermonecrotic toxin alone triggered typical inflammatory cell accumulation (Fig. 7B-2 and 7B-5) and edema signals (Fig. 7A, 7B-5), as well as collagen fiber disorganization (Fig. 7B-5) and presence of fibrin in connective tissue (Fig. 7B-5).

the injection of recombinant hyaluronidase alone, PBS/BSA and a recombinant protein not related to hyaluronic acid hydrolysis (negative controls) did not show macroscopic erythema, ecchymosis or dermonecrosis (Fig. 6A and Fig.6B).
334.Rationale: The passage shows that dermonecrotic toxin (phospholipase-D) alone can induce dermonecrosis, and hyaluronidase alone does not cause lesions, indicating hyaluronidase is not essential for the toxin's dermonecrotic activity. There is no evidence in the passage that hyaluronidase activates phospholipase-D; instead, it enhances the spread of toxins.

334.Option: The hyaluronidase from Loxosceles venom contributes to dermonecrotic injury by directly degrading collagen fibers and disrupting blood vessel walls in the dermis.
Evidence: Through analysis with CS, DS and HS at a substrate-to-enzyme ratio of 1∶1, we noted that recombinant hyaluronidase degraded CS (Fig. 5B, lane 3) and did not degrade DS and HS (data not shown), similar to the native hyaluronidases from venom.
By degrading glycosaminoglycans, the hyaluronidase reduces the viscosity of hyaluronic acid and renders the extracellular matrix less rigid.
334.Rationale: Hyaluronidase is shown to degrade glycosaminoglycans like hyaluronic acid and chondroitin sulfate, but there is no evidence it directly degrades collagen fibers or disrupts blood vessel walls. Histological findings indicate disorganization of the extracellular matrix, but this is likely due to enhanced toxin spread rather than direct enzymatic action on collagen.

334.Option: The hyaluronidase from Loxosceles venom acts as a spreading factor that enhances the diffusion of other venom toxins, such as phospholipase-D, through the extracellular matrix, thereby increasing the area of dermonecrotic lesions and promoting gravitational spreading of skin injury.
Evidence: The idea that brown spider venom hyaluronidases play a role in the gravitational spreading of dermonecrosis and/or systemic diffusion of venom toxins, and then act as 'spreading factors' comes from its degradative activity on hyaluronic acid and other glycosaminoglycans that mediate tissue integrity and stability.

By degrading glycosaminoglycans, the hyaluronidase reduces the viscosity of hyaluronic acid and renders the extracellular matrix less rigid. This change makes the matrix more permeable to other toxins and facilitates the spread of other venom constituents and inflammatory cell mediators.

The length of dermonecrosis lesions in the rabbit skin of Dietrich's Hyaluronidase concomitantly injected with dermonecrotic toxin was substantially larger than for dermonecrotic toxin alone after 24 h (Fig. 6B).
The macroscopic gravitational spreading and edema induced by the two recombinant enzymes injected together were very similar to whole venom (Fig. 6C).
334.Rationale: The passage explicitly describes hyaluronidase as a spreading factor that degrades extracellular matrix components to facilitate toxin diffusion. Experimental results confirm that hyaluronidase, when combined with dermonecrotic toxin (phospholipase-D), increases lesion size and promotes gravitational spreading, similar to whole venom effects.

334.Option: The hyaluronidase from Loxosceles venom functions as a major allergen that triggers anaphylactic reactions in susceptible individuals, contributing to the systemic symptoms observed in loxoscelism.
Evidence: Systemic involvement has also been reported including fever, malaise, weakness, nausea, vomiting and in severe cases, intravascular coagulation, hemolysis and acute renal disturbance.
334.Rationale: While systemic symptoms are mentioned, the passage does not attribute them to allergic reactions or anaphylaxis caused by hyaluronidase. The role of hyaluronidase is described in terms of enzymatic activity and toxin spread, not allergenicity.

334.Option: The hyaluronidase from Loxosceles venom is primarily responsible for the direct induction of dermonecrotic lesions and systemic toxicity in brown spider bites.
Evidence: the injection of recombinant hyaluronidase alone, PBS/BSA and a recombinant protein not related to hyaluronic acid hydrolysis (negative controls) did not show macroscopic erythema, ecchymosis or dermonecrosis (Fig. 6A and Fig.6B).

Dermonecrotic toxin alone triggered typical inflammatory cell accumulation (Fig. 7B-2 and 7B-5) and edema signals (Fig. 7A, 7B-5), as well as collagen fiber disorganization (Fig. 7B-5) and presence of fibrin in connective tissue (Fig. 7B-5).
334.Rationale: Hyaluronidase alone does not induce dermonecrotic lesions; it requires dermonecrotic toxins like phospholipase-D to cause injury. Thus, it is not primarily responsible for direct lesion induction or systemic toxicity; its role is to enhance the spread of other toxins.

335.Option: The higher odds of cysticercosis in breeding female pigs compared to males indicates that reproductive status directly increases susceptibility to Taenia spp. infection.
Evidence: breeding sows had significantly higher odds of antigen presence compared to male pigs (OR = 10.35, p = 0.01)
335.Rationale: The passage reports an association between breeding sow status and higher odds of Taenia spp. antigen presence in pigs, but it does not establish causation or provide evidence that reproductive status directly increases biological susceptibility. The observed association could be due to behavioral, management, or other confounding factors. The passage does not support a direct causal inference.

335.Option: Environmental factors, including land cover and precipitation, influence the spatial distribution of Taenia spp. infections in humans and pigs, with flooding agricultural and grassland areas showing a non-linear association with cysticercosis risk.
Evidence: The percentage of land that was flooding agricultural or grassland had a quadratic relationship with presence of antigen in humans: lower odds of antigen presence were associated with very low or very high percentages of this land cover class, while the odds of antigen presence were highest in areas with intermediate percentages of the land cover class (OR = 1.09 and 0.998; p = 0.03 and 0.03 for the linear and squared terms respectively).
Precipitation was negatively associated with the outcome (OR = 0.998, p = 0.02).
flooding agricultural land and grassland demonstrated a positive association with the outcome (OR = 1.04, p = 0.004) for pigs.

This research aimed to assess the hypothesis that spatial clustering of cysticercosis is the result of a combination of (a) localised transmission cycles giving rise to spatial aggregations and (b) the impact of environmental conditions on egg survival and, thus, onward transmission.
335.Rationale: The passage explicitly tests and supports the role of environmental factors in the spatial distribution of Taenia spp. infections. The results show significant associations between land cover (flooding agricultural and grassland) and precipitation with cysticercosis in both humans and pigs. For humans, the relationship with flooding agricultural and grassland is non-linear (quadratic), as stated. This directly supports the inference that environmental conditions influence spatial patterns of infection.

335.Option: The spatial clustering of cysticercosis cases in households near tapeworm carriers is the primary driver of infection risk, overshadowing the influence of environmental factors.
Evidence: Spatial analyses have demonstrated significant clustering of taeniasis, porcine cysticercosis and human cysticercosis, with evidence of aggregation of human and porcine cysticercosis cases within close proximity to human tapeworm carriers.

The results from the multivariable human model for presence of Taenia spp. antigen in humans (Table 4) indicate... The percentage of land that was flooding agricultural or grassland had a quadratic relationship... Precipitation was negatively associated...
The final model for presence of Taenia spp. antigen in pigs (Table 5) indicated... flooding agricultural land and grassland demonstrated a positive association with the outcome.
335.Rationale: While the passage acknowledges spatial clustering near tapeworm carriers, the study specifically aimed to assess the additional role of environmental conditions. The results show that environmental factors (land cover, precipitation) are significantly associated with infection risk in multivariable models, indicating they contribute independently. The passage does not compare the relative importance or suggest that clustering near carriers overshadows environmental factors; in fact, it demonstrates that environmental factors are influential.

335.Option: The lack of correlation between microscopy and copro-antigen ELISA results in this study suggests that the copro-antigen ELISA is unreliable for detecting taeniasis, and thus, the observed spatial patterns in cysticercosis may be influenced by diagnostic inaccuracies.
Evidence: The correlation between the two diagnostic methods for taeniasis (microscopy and copro-antigen ELISA) was zero: four participants were positive for taeniasis via microscopy only, 397 were positive for taeniasis via copro-antigen detection only and none were positive using both methods.

Based on the lack of correlation between the two diagnostic methods, and unexpectedly high number of positive results, the taeniasis results were not used in further statistical analyses as a precautionary measure.
The prevalence of Taenia spp. antigen (suggestive of cysticercosis) was 6.6% in humans (95% CI: 5.6%–7.7%) and 17.2% in pigs (95% CI: 10.2%–26.4%).
335.Rationale: The passage notes discordance between two taeniasis diagnostic methods and consequently excludes taeniasis data from analysis. However, the spatial patterns for cysticercosis are based on HP10 antigen ELISA detection in humans and pigs, which is a separate diagnostic. The passage does not suggest that the cysticercosis diagnostic is unreliable or that its inaccuracies influence the observed spatial patterns. The inference that cysticercosis patterns may be influenced by diagnostic inaccuracies is not supported by evidence in the passage.

335.Option: The negative association between precipitation and cysticercosis risk suggests that rainfall reduces environmental contamination, thereby decreasing transmission of Taenia spp. eggs.
Evidence: Precipitation was negatively associated with the outcome (OR = 0.998, p = 0.02).

Surface moisture and humidity are thought to be the main constraining factors for Taenia spp. eggs in the environment: the eggs are vulnerable to desiccation and survival is greatly reduced under dry conditions, regardless of temperature.
335.Rationale: The passage reports a negative statistical association between precipitation and human cysticercosis risk, but it does not provide evidence for the causal mechanism proposed (that rainfall reduces environmental contamination). In fact, the passage suggests that moisture and humidity favor egg survival (reducing desiccation). The observed association might reflect complex environmental dynamics not explained in the text. The causal claim about reduced contamination and transmission is not derivable from the passage; only the statistical association is reported.

336.Option: The high confidence in domestically-made vaccines and the preference for them over foreign-made vaccines indicate that public trust in China’s vaccine regulatory system has been fully restored after past scandals.
Evidence: In China, vaccine hesitancy is a complex public health issue. In the last decade, vaccine scandals and a series of reports about the serious side-effects of vaccination have increased vaccination hesitancy and distrust in the country’s immunization program.
The vast majority of the study participants reported that they were confident (48.7%, 95%CI 47.0 to 50.3) or completely confident (46.1%, 95%CI 44.5 to 47.8) in domestically-made COVID-19 vaccines.

Findings on the preference of domestically-made over foreign-made COVID-19 vaccines revealed that 64.2% (95%CI 62.6 to 65.8) reported a preference for domestically-made and 11.9% (95%CI 10.8 to 13.0) preferred foreign-made COVID-19 vaccines.
336.Rationale: The passage presents data showing current confidence and preference for domestically-made vaccines, but it also notes past scandals and ongoing vaccine hesitancy. The inference that trust has been 'fully restored' is not supported by the evidence; the data reflect current survey responses but do not establish a full restoration of trust, especially given the context of prior distrust and the fact that only 28.7% expressed a definite vaccination intent.

336.Option: The perception of susceptibility to COVID-19 was not a significant predictor of vaccination intention, indicating that public health campaigns should focus on increasing fear of infection to drive vaccine uptake.
Evidence: The participants had low perceptions of susceptibility. The majority disagreed that there was a great chance of getting COVID-19 in the next few months (68.7%), were also not worried about the likelihood of getting COVID-19 (56.8%), and disagreed that it was currently possible that they would get COVID-19 (72.0%).
Most of the constructs in the HBM model were significantly associated with having a definite intention for COVID-19 vaccination in the univariate analysis.
336.Rationale: While participants had low perceived susceptibility, the passage does not state that perceived susceptibility was not a significant predictor of vaccination intention. The univariate analysis indicates most constructs were significant, so susceptibility may have been significant. Moreover, the passage does not suggest that public health campaigns should focus on increasing fear; such a recommendation is not derived from the evidence provided.

336.Option: The significant positive moderating effect of income level on the relationship between perceived susceptibility, severity, and cues to action with WTP suggests that public health interventions should prioritize higher-income groups to maximize vaccine uptake.
Evidence: A significant positive moderating effect of income level on the relationship perceived susceptibility and WTP was found (β = 3.007, p = 0.045). Income level was also found significantly moderated the relationship between perceived severity and WTP (β = 3.980, p = 0.026).
Income level showed a strong statistically significant moderating effect on the relationship between cues to action (β = 0.313, p = 0.551) and WTP.
336.Rationale: Income level had a significant moderating effect on the relationship between perceived susceptibility and severity with WTP, but for cues to action, the p-value of 0.551 indicates no significant moderating effect. The inference that public health interventions should prioritize higher-income groups is not supported by the passage; the study examines WTP, not necessarily vaccine uptake, and does not make such a recommendation. The evidence does not suggest prioritizing any income group for maximizing uptake.

336.Option: The fact that only 28.7% of respondents reported a definite intention to vaccinate suggests that the majority of the population in China is unwilling to accept a COVID-19 vaccine, necessitating mandatory vaccination policies.
Evidence: On the whole, a total of 2,950 (83.5%; 95%CI 82.3–84.8) participants responded yes to COVID-19 vaccine intent, while only 582 (16.4%; 95%CI 15.2–17.7) responded no. By a more specific breakdown, the majority responded probably yes (54.8%; 95% CI 53.2–56.5) followed by definitely yes (28.7%; 95% CI 27.3–30.3%).
336.Rationale: The data show that 83.5% of respondents expressed some intention to vaccinate (probably yes or definitely yes), indicating that the majority is willing, not unwilling. The statement that the majority is unwilling is incorrect. Additionally, the passage does not discuss or imply the necessity of mandatory vaccination policies; such an inference is not derived from the evidence.

336.Option: Despite concerns about the safety and efficacy of the vaccine, the perception of benefits and the provision of adequate information were strong predictors of a definite intention to receive the COVID-19 vaccine.
Evidence: Under the perceived barriers construct, concerns about the COVID-19 vaccine being faulty/fake and affordability were reported by 82.0% and 75.1%, respectively. Concerns about safety (72.6%) and efficacy (71.2%) were also notable.
Perception that vaccination decreases the chances of getting COVID-19 under the perceived benefit construct (OR = 3.14, 95% CI 2.05–4.83) was the strongest predictor for a definite intention.
Many of the participants (92.1%) reported that they would only receive the COVID-19 vaccine if given adequate information.
336.Rationale: While concerns about safety and efficacy are noted and perceived benefits are a strong predictor (with an OR provided), the passage does not provide evidence that 'the provision of adequate information' was a strong predictor of definite intention. The high percentage of participants wanting adequate information indicates its importance as a condition, but it is not shown to be a predictor in the regression analysis. The only cue to action predictor mentioned is about the vaccine being taken by many in the public (OR=1.88). Therefore, the statement is not fully supported by the evidence.

337.Option: The multivariate regression model incorporating HI and socioeconomic/climatic variables is superior to simulation models in predicting DHF incidence, suggesting that real-world data can fully capture the dynamics of antibody-dependent enhancement.
Evidence: The predictability attained solely by HI was much inferior to that attained in any simulations (Figure 8).
However, the predictability using the multivariate regression model was as good as that in simulations with cross-immunity, up to W = 8.
337.Rationale: The passage states that the multivariate model's predictability is only as good as simulations with cross-immunity for certain averaging windows (up to W=8), not superior overall. It does not support that real-world data fully capture antibody-dependent enhancement dynamics; the simulations incorporate cross-immunity mechanisms, and the comparison shows complex patterns not fully explained by the multivariate model.

337.Option: The observed non-monotonic relationship between DHF incidence and HI is primarily driven by age-dependent differences in the probability of manifesting severe illness, independent of cross-serotype immunity.
Evidence: GAM detected a non-monotonic relationship in the simulations with cross-immunity (Figure 7A), a negative relationship in those with age-dependency (Figure 7E), and a slightly positive relationship in the control simulations (Figure 7I).
Univariate analysis of GAM revealed that the correlation between HI and incidence was positive below about HI = 30, while the correlation was negative above this HI value (Figure 2B; Figure 3).
337.Rationale: The non-monotonic relationship (positive below HI=30, negative above) is observed empirically. Simulations show that cross-immunity produces a non-monotonic pattern, while age-dependency produces a negative relationship, not non-monotonic. Therefore, the non-monotonic relationship is more consistent with cross-serotype immunity, not primarily age-dependency independent of cross-immunity.

337.Option: The negative response of DHF incidence to transmission intensity at areas of intense transmission has important public health implications, regardless of its underlying mechanism.
Evidence: a mathematical model recently predicted that, due to the presence of transient cross-serotype immunity, the incidence of DHF and transmission intensity will be correlated negatively at high transmission intensities [22].
Univariate analysis of GAM revealed that the correlation between HI and incidence was positive below about HI = 30, while the correlation was negative above this HI value (Figure 2B; Figure 3).
Our findings have major implications for future epidemiological surveys and dengue control programs.
337.Rationale: The passage explicitly states that findings have major implications for dengue control programs. The observed negative correlation between DHF incidence and vector abundance (HI) at high transmission intensity (HI>30) is a key empirical result. Epidemiological reasoning indicates that such a relationship would impact vector control strategies, as reducing vector abundance in high-transmission areas might not simply reduce DHF incidence and could even have counterintuitive effects. The implication is derived from the observed pattern and the stated relevance to control programs.

337.Option: Since HI is strongly correlated with vector abundance, reducing HI to below 30 will inevitably lead to a significant reduction in DHF incidence across all transmission settings.
Evidence: Univariate analysis of GAM revealed that the correlation between HI and incidence was positive below about HI = 30, while the correlation was negative above this HI value (Figure 2B; Figure 3).
as HI decreases from 70 to 30, log incidence would increase by 0.6 (Figure 4A), which corresponds to an increase of 80%.
337.Rationale: The relationship between HI and DHF incidence is non-monotonic: positive below HI=30 and negative above. Reducing HI from above 30 to below 30 might initially increase incidence as HI approaches 30 (since incidence peaks around HI=30), not inevitably reduce it. Moreover, 'inevitably' implies a deterministic effect, but the passage shows statistical associations with variability. The effect depends on the starting HI and other factors, so the claim is not supported.

337.Option: The high variability in DHF incidence across districts is primarily due to differences in the number of public large water wells, which serve as the main breeding sites for Aedes mosquitoes.
Evidence: Although incorporation of socioeconomic/variables improved the goodness of fit, this multivariate predictive model still failed to reproduce the very wide variation in the observed incidence (compare Figure 2B vs Figure 5).
337.Rationale: The multivariate model, which includes public large wells as one variable, could not fully explain the high variability in DHF incidence. Thus, public large wells are not the primary cause. Additionally, the passage does not identify public large wells as the main breeding sites; it mentions water-holding containers generally, and public large wells are just one of several socioeconomic factors considered.

338.Option: The 'valley of death' in diagnostic development is primarily caused by insufficient funding for large-scale prospective studies, which prevents promising early-phase candidates from being validated in real-world settings.
338.Rationale: The passage does not discuss funding issues or the concept of a 'valley of death.' It describes the distribution of tests across evaluation phases but provides no causal explanation for why many candidates do not advance. Therefore, this inference cannot be derived from the passage.

338.Option: The diagnostic accuracy of rK28-based immunochromatographic tests in East Africa is consistently high across multiple studies, suggesting that this antigen is a reliable candidate for widespread deployment in the region.
Evidence: RDTs with rK28 antigen have been evaluated in four studies in Sudan and Ethiopia with promising results for the test manufactured by CTK Biotech (sensitivities between 94 and 98%, and specificities between 95 and 98%) while sensitivities and specificities based on RDTs by other manufacturers and in ELISA were more variable (sensitivities between 89 and 100%, and specificities between 81 and 99%) [13–17].

With rk28 having been evaluated on a relatively small number of specimens (VL suspected patients: 285), there is a risk that its performance turns out to be variable based on the population it is evaluated on.

Only Indirect Fluorescent Antibody Technique (IFAT) and the rK28 ICT were shown to be >94% sensitive and >97% specific when evaluated in phase III studies on respectively 104 and 285 East African persons [15,108].
338.Rationale: While the passage reports high diagnostic accuracy for rK28-based tests from one manufacturer in specific studies, it also highlights variability across manufacturers and populations, and notes the small sample size. The passage cautions about potential variability and emphasizes the need for further investigation of alternative antigens, rather than concluding that rK28 is reliable for widespread deployment. Additionally, the question asks for a general pattern in diagnostic development, not the performance of a specific antigen.

338.Option: The diagnostic development pipeline for visceral leishmaniasis serodiagnostic tests is characterized by a high proportion of early-phase evaluations, with most candidates not advancing to large-scale prospective studies despite showing high diagnostic accuracy in initial assessments.
Evidence: Forty four tests representing 42 antigens are evaluated on ≤100 specimens in phase I studies, 57 tests representing 53 antigens were evaluated on >100-specimens in phase II studies, 10 tests representing 9 antigens, were evaluated in phase III studies. Thus 86 out of the 96 tests (89%) were evaluated in phase I and/or II only.

Seventeen of the tests in phase I, 11 in phase II and 1 test in phase III (rK28 immunochromatographic test [ICT] from CTK) consistently have sensitivities above 94% and specificities above 97% in all studies analysed.
338.Rationale: These quotes directly show that the vast majority of tests (89%) are evaluated only in early phases (I and II), and many of these early-phase tests demonstrate high diagnostic accuracy (e.g., 17 in phase I and 11 in phase II meet the high accuracy thresholds). However, only one test in phase III meets those criteria, indicating that most candidates do not progress to large-scale prospective studies despite promising early results.

338.Option: The majority of serodiagnostic tests for visceral leishmaniasis are not advanced to phase III because they fail to meet minimum diagnostic accuracy thresholds in early-phase evaluations.
Evidence: Seventeen of the tests in phase I, 11 in phase II and 1 test in phase III (rK28 immunochromatographic test [ICT] from CTK) consistently have sensitivities above 94% and specificities above 97% in all studies analysed.
338.Rationale: This quote shows that many tests in early phases (17 in phase I and 11 in phase II) do meet the high accuracy thresholds (sensitivity >94% and specificity >97%). Therefore, failure to meet accuracy thresholds is not the primary reason why most tests are not advanced to phase III. The passage does not provide an alternative explanation, but the data contradict the claim in this option.

338.Option: The lack of standardized production methods for native antigens with carbohydrate and lipid moieties contributes to high batch-to-batch variation, which limits their potential for commercialization and widespread use in low-resource settings.
338.Rationale: The passage classifies antigens based on the presence of carbohydrate and lipid moieties and discusses whether they are native or defined, but it does not mention issues with standardized production methods, batch-to-batch variation, or how these factors affect commercialization and use in low-resource settings. Therefore, this inference cannot be derived from the passage.

339.Option: Participants who reported trying to do as many physical and social activities as possible were more likely to experience a faster recovery and return to normal functioning, suggesting that active coping strategies are clinically effective.
Evidence: The participants using emotional coping strategies went less often to a physician, used less medications and reported trying to do as many physical/social activities as possible.
With this in mind, people were more willing to accept that symptoms would eventually subside.

The study is qualitative and focuses on self-reported behaviors and perceptions, not on clinical outcomes such as faster recovery. Therefore, this inference cannot be derived from the Passage Body.

339.Option: Since effective long-term treatments are not available yet for chronic chikungunya, the high uptake of (alternative) medicines and frequent physician visits will normally not lead to success, resulting in dissatisfaction and further emotional stress.
Evidence: As participants were aware that there was no cure for chikungunya, this left them wondering if their pain and other symptoms would ever fade away.

Frequent visits of health care providers as coping strategy led to unmet expectations for cure or treatment, resulting in stress (negative impact on QoL).
339.Rationale: The Passage Body indicates that there is no cure for chikungunya, and that problem-focused coping strategies like high uptake of medicines and frequent physician visits often lead to unmet expectations, dissatisfaction, and emotional stress. This inference is directly supported by the described patterns of health-seeking behavior and coping mechanisms.

339.Option: The universal health care program in Curaçao minimized out-of-pocket expenditures and reduced the financial burden on individuals, which may have contributed to higher healthcare utilization among chikungunya patients.
Evidence: In Curaçao, chikungunya patients could rely on an insurance system, which covered health care costs, which in turn minimized out-of-pocket expenditures.
The financial impact due to chikungunya on participant’s personal situation was generally perceived as limited.
339.Rationale: While the Passage Body states that insurance minimized out-of-pocket expenditures and reduced financial burden, it does not provide any evidence linking this to higher healthcare utilization. Healthcare utilization is discussed in the context of coping strategies (e.g., problem-focused coping leading to frequent visits), but not in relation to insurance coverage. Therefore, this inference cannot be derived from the Passage Body.

339.Option: Problem-focused coping strategies aimed at physical healing induced a high uptake of (alternative) medicines and consultation of physicians, whereas emotional coping strategies focused on acceptance of the situation were linked to lower perceived impacts on HRQoL and less uptake of medical care.
Evidence: Within the community, problem-focused coping was commonly performed by using (natural) medicines and by visiting a physician to cope with the physical impact of chikungunya.

In contrast, people with lingering complaints while reporting less impact of chikungunya also showed different coping strategies, i.e. emotional coping strategies. The participants using emotional coping strategies went less often to a physician, used less medications and reported trying to do as many physical/social activities as possible.
With this in mind, people were more willing to accept that symptoms would eventually subside.
339.Rationale: The Passage Body explicitly describes that problem-focused coping strategies involve high healthcare utilization (use of medicines and physician visits), while emotional coping strategies involve acceptance, lower healthcare utilization, and are associated with reporting less impact on HRQoL. This inference is directly derivable from the evidence presented.

340.Option: The increase in the median age of hospitalized dengue cases over time indicates that younger patients are being under-treated or under-referred to hospitals.
Evidence: the median age among hospitalized cases increased from 38 years in 2007 to 43 years in 2017 (p < 0.0005)

older adults (aged ≥45 years) accounted for an increasingly higher proportion over the 15-year period: from 21.0% to 29.9% in the age group 45–64 years, and from 4.9% to 16.8% in elderly patients ≥65 years of age (all p < 0.0005)
340.Rationale: The increase in median age of hospitalized cases is documented, but the passage attributes this to more selective admission criteria focusing on higher-risk groups, including older adults and those with comorbidities. There is no evidence that younger patients are being under-treated or under-referred; rather, the change reflects targeted hospitalization based on risk.

340.Option: The reduction in hospitalization rates for dengue cases is primarily due to improved early diagnosis and management in primary care settings, which has led to fewer patients requiring hospital admission.
Evidence: the proportion of dengue cases hospitalized plummeted from 97.3% in 2003 to an all-time low of 25.6% in 2014 (p < 0.0005)
In the aftermath of the 2004–2005 dengue epidemic in Singapore, hospitals reviewed and refined their admission criteria for dengue cases

MOH sent out circulars to hospitals, government primary care clinics and medical practitioners to apprise them of the dengue situation during epidemic periods, and to provide periodic updates on guidelines for the management of dengue

No evidence is provided that primary care improvements were the primary driver.

340.Option: The implementation of more selective admission criteria for dengue has not led to an increase in adverse outcomes, despite a significant reduction in the proportion of cases hospitalized.
Evidence: the proportion of dengue cases hospitalized plummeted from 97.3% in 2003 to an all-time low of 25.6% in 2014 (p < 0.0005)
The CFR ranged from 0% in 2017 to 0.27% in 2006.
About 0.6% to 1.5% of dengue patients in hospital were admitted to the ICU during the study period.
The proportion of deaths among hospitalized patients ranged from 0.14% in 2003 to 0.77% in 2009.
340.Rationale: The data show a dramatic decline in hospitalization proportions over the study period, while case fatality rates and ICU admission proportions remained low and did not exhibit an increasing trend. This supports the inference that more selective admission criteria did not result in increased adverse outcomes.

340.Option: The proportion of dengue cases hospitalized in the elderly aged ≥65 years has been highest since 2013, which may be attributed to both the MOH guideline to refer for hospital evaluation those aged ≥65 years as well as a shift in demographic profile of dengue cases towards older adults.
Evidence: the highest proportions were in older adults aged 45–64 years from 2003 to 2012 (range: 50.8% to 100.0%) and elderly persons aged ≥65 years from 2013 to 2017 (range: 47.2% to 55.4%)
elderly patients with medical co-morbidities who are unwell
the median age of dengue cases remained about the same from 2007 onwards
340.Rationale: While the proportion of hospitalizations in the elderly has been high since 2013 and guidelines include elderly patients, the passage does not support a shift in the demographic profile of dengue cases toward older adults; the median age of dengue cases remained stable, indicating no such shift.

340.Option: The implementation of more selective admission criteria by hospitals has led to a significant reduction in hospital bed utilization, even during large dengue epidemics.
Evidence: The total number of hospital bed-years due to dengue halved from 136 in 2005 to 69 in 2013 (Fig 4).
the proportion of dengue cases hospitalized plummeted from 97.3% in 2003 to an all-time low of 25.6% in 2014 (p < 0.0005)
The proportion of dengue cases hospitalized during these three epidemic periods was 93.2% in 2004–2005, 58.1% in 2007 and 28.9% in 2013–2014.
340.Rationale: The data show a halving of hospital bed-years and a steep decline in hospitalization proportions, including during large epidemics (e.g., 2013-2014), coinciding with the implementation of more selective admission criteria. This directly supports the inference that bed utilization decreased significantly as a result of the policy change.

341.Option: The decline in anti-filarial IgG4 antibody prevalence after MDA occurred more slowly than the decline in microfilaria prevalence, indicating that antibody levels persist longer after infection clearance and may reflect past exposure rather than current transmission.
Evidence: The antibody to MF ratio was 3.1 at baseline, but increased dramatically after MDA. This means MF rates fell more rapidly after MDA than antibody rates.

Anti-filarial antibodies are a marker for past, present or exposure to filarial infections. Antibody clearance tends to occur faster after treatment in children than in adults, since children tend to have shorter term exposure and lighter infections. Therefore, adults may have persistent antibodies years after effective therapy.
341.Rationale: The Passage explicitly states that microfilaria (MF) rates fell more rapidly than antibody rates after MDA, directly supporting that antibody decline is slower. It also explains that anti-filarial antibodies can persist after effective therapy and reflect past exposure, which aligns with the inference that antibodies may indicate past exposure rather than current transmission.

341.Option: The increase in the antibody-to-MF ratio after MDA reflects a higher sensitivity of the Brugia Rapid test in detecting past infections compared to the membrane filtration method used for microfilariae.
341.Rationale: The Passage explains the increase in the antibody-to-MF ratio as due to MF rates falling more rapidly than antibody rates, not due to differences in test sensitivity. While the Brugia Rapid test is described as sensitive, there is no comparison of its sensitivity for past infections versus membrane filtration for microfilariae. The inference about higher sensitivity for past infections is not supported by the Passage.

341.Option: The persistence of anti-filarial antibodies in children after MDA cessation indicates that transmission of B. timori was not fully interrupted in the study population.
341.Rationale: Antibody rates in children declined to low levels (e.g., below 5% in children aged 3-10 years by 2009), and the Passage suggests that remaining antibodies might be from past exposure during early MDA years. MDA was discontinued because MF rates were below 1% for two years, indicating interrupted transmission, and only one non-compliant individual had persistent microfilaria. There is no evidence from the Passage that antibody persistence in children implies ongoing transmission.

341.Option: The rebound in STH infection rates after MDA cessation suggests that anti-filarial antibodies may also reappear in the population if transmission resumes.
341.Rationale: The Passage discusses soil-transmitted helminth (STH) infection rates rebounding after MDA cessation, but this is separate from filarial antibodies. There is no inference or evidence in the Passage connecting STH dynamics to the reappearance of anti-filarial antibodies if filarial transmission resumes. The option involves an unsupported cross-parasite comparison not derivable from the Passage.

341.Option: The rapid decline in microfilaria prevalence following MDA suggests that the Brugia Rapid test is a more sensitive marker for active infection than for past exposure.
341.Rationale: The rapid decline in MF prevalence is attributed to the efficacy of MDA, not the sensitivity of the antibody test. The Passage indicates that antibody rates decline more slowly than MF rates, suggesting that the Brugia Rapid test (which detects antibodies) is more reflective of past exposure rather than active infection. The inference in the option contradicts the Passage's explanation that antibody rates persist longer after MF clearance.

342.Option: The high level of amino acid variation (36.4%) in mitochondrial protein-coding genes between human-Trichuris and pig-Trichuris is consistent with recent cross-infection events between the two host species.
Evidence: For individual genes, the nucleotide and amino acid sequence differences between human-Trichuris and pig-Trichuris vary from 25.4 to 37.4% and 13.6 to 62.5%, respectively (Table 5).
Sequence difference across the entire mt genome was 32.87%.
Phylogenetic analyses of amino acid sequence datasets using B. malayi as the outgroup reflected the clear genetic distinctiveness between human-Trichuris and pig-Trichuris
342.Rationale: The passage reports substantial amino acid sequence differences (13.6–62.5%) and nucleotide divergence (32.87% across the mt genome), which indicate genetic divergence, not similarity. Recent cross-infection events would be expected to reduce genetic divergence due to gene flow. The phylogenetic analysis supports clear genetic distinctiveness, which contradicts the idea of recent cross-infection.

342.Option: The genetic similarity in the rrnL gene between human-Trichuris and pig-Trichuris suggests that they are closely related and likely represent a single, host-generalist species.
Evidence: Comparison of the mt genomes of human-Trichuris and pig-Trichuris showed that the rrnS and rrnL were the two most conserved genes (Table 5).

Sequence variation in part of the rrnL gene was assessed among 16 individuals of Trichuris from humans and pigs... The alignment of the partial rrnL sequences revealed that all individuals of human-Trichuris differed at 89 nucleotide positions when compared with pig-Trichuris.
Phylogenetic analyses of the rrnL sequence data revealed strong support for the separation of human-Trichuris from pig-Trichuris individuals into two distinct clades
342.Rationale: While rrnL is noted as one of the most conserved genes, the passage still reports significant nucleotide differences (25.1% variation in rrnL) and 89 nucleotide differences between human and pig Trichuris in the partial rrnL alignment. Phylogenetic analyses strongly support separation into two distinct clades, contradicting the claim that they represent a single, host-generalist species.

342.Option: The substantial genetic divergence observed in mitochondrial protein-coding genes and ribosomal RNA genes between human-Trichuris and pig-Trichuris supports the hypothesis that they represent distinct species.
Evidence: Therefore, in the present study, we (i) characterised the mt genomes of human-derived Trichuris and pig-derived Trichuris, (ii) compared these genomes and (iii) then tested the hypothesis that human-Trichuris and pig-Trichuris are genetically distinct
Sequence difference across the entire mt genome was 32.87%.
The amino acid sequence differences ranged from 13.6–62.5%, with COX1 being the most conserved protein, and ATP8 the least conserved.
Phylogenetic analyses of amino acid sequence datasets using B. malayi as the outgroup reflected the clear genetic distinctiveness between human-Trichuris and pig-Trichuris
Phylogenetic analyses of the rrnL sequence data revealed strong support for the separation of human-Trichuris from pig-Trichuris individuals into two distinct clades
342.Rationale: The passage explicitly states the study's goal was to test the hypothesis of genetic distinctness. Results show high sequence divergence across the mt genome (32.87%), substantial amino acid differences (13.6–62.5%), and phylogenetic analyses with strong support for separation into two clades. This molecular evidence directly supports the inference that human-Trichuris and pig-Trichuris represent distinct species.

342.Option: The high A+T content and AT-rich non-coding regions in the mitochondrial genomes of human-Trichuris and pig-Trichuris indicate that these parasites have evolved a shared mechanism for mitochondrial DNA replication.
Evidence: The nucleotide composition of the entire mt genome is biased toward A and T, with T being the most favoured nucleotide and G being least favoured
Two AT-rich non-coding regions (NCRs) were inferred in the mt genomes of both human-Trichuris and pig-Trichuris

Although nothing is yet known about the replication process in the mt DNA of parasitic nematodes, the high A+T content and the predicted structure of the AT-rich NCRs suggest an involvement in the initiation of replication [41]
342.Rationale: The passage notes high A+T content and AT-rich non-coding regions in both genomes and suggests these features may be involved in replication initiation, but it explicitly states that nothing is yet known about the replication process. The presence of similar features does not demonstrate that they have evolved a shared mechanism; it could be a common ancestral trait. The passage does not provide evidence for a shared derived mechanism specific to these two parasites.

342.Option: The use of mitochondrial DNA markers in this study provides definitive proof that Trichuris from humans and pigs are reproductively isolated and cannot interbreed.
Evidence: Phylogenetic analyses of amino acid sequence datasets using B. malayi as the outgroup reflected the clear genetic distinctiveness between human-Trichuris and pig-Trichuris
Sequence difference across the entire mt genome was 32.87%.
342.Rationale: While the mtDNA data show clear genetic distinctiveness, reproductive isolation is a different criterion for species delineation that requires evidence from crossing experiments or population genetic studies. The passage does not address interbreeding or reproductive isolation; it only provides molecular genetic evidence of divergence. Thus, the claim of 'definitive proof' of reproductive isolation overstates the evidence presented.

343.Option: The cumulative negative conversion rate of blood-cultured fungi is significantly influenced by the duration of antifungal therapy, with longer treatment leading to better clearance regardless of fungal susceptibility.
Evidence: Strains from the 190 patients were split into three groups according to their MIC values for voriconazole in vitro: lower (MIC≤0.008), medium (0.008<VOA≤0.015), and higher (VOA>0.015). And cumulative analysis to assess negative conversion rate of blood cultured fungi was carried out among the three groups after 2, 4, 6 weeks and more than 6 weeks of antifungal treatment.

The cumulative negative conversion rate of blood cultured fungi among three groups after 2, 4, 6 weeks and more than 6 weeks of antifungal treatment was significantly different (Shown in Fig 2), indicating that the MIC values of voriconazole significantly affects the clearance of T. marneffei in blood.
343.Rationale: The passage presents cumulative negative conversion rates over time but specifically in relation to voriconazole MIC groups, showing significant differences among groups. This indicates that clearance depends on fungal susceptibility (MIC), not merely on treatment duration. The passage does not support the claim that longer treatment leads to better clearance regardless of susceptibility; rather, it demonstrates that susceptibility influences clearance rates at each time point.

343.Option: The susceptibility of T. marneffei to voriconazole in vitro is an independent factor influencing the clearance of the fungus from blood culture after antifungal therapy in AIDS patients with talaromycosis.
Evidence: Significant differences were found in the overall distribution of MIC value between the two groups both for VOR (Z = 1.762, P < 0.001) and FLU (Z = 1.211, P = 0.006). The MICs of VOR and FLU in the Pos-group were significantly higher than those in the Neg-group (the proportion of isolates with MICs above 0.008 for VOR: 69/101 (68.3%) vs. 38/89 (42.7%), respectively, χ2 = 12.623, P < 0.001; the proportion of isolates with MICs above 4 for FLU: 28/101 (27.7%) vs. 9/89 (10.1%), respectively, χ2 = 9.356, P = 0.002)

Multivariate analysis using the logistic regression model involving the 2 significant factors (AST and CD4+T levels)determined by univariate analysis and the MIC values for VOR and FLU. The MIC value for VOR was identified as the prognostic variable derived from binary logistic regression analysis that influence the clearance of T. marneffei in blood culture after antifungal therapy among AIDS patients with talaromycosis (shown in Table 4).

The cumulative negative conversion rate of blood cultured fungi among three groups after 2, 4, 6 weeks and more than 6 weeks of antifungal treatment was significantly different (Shown in Fig 2), indicating that the MIC values of voriconazole significantly affects the clearance of T. marneffei in blood.
343.Rationale: The passage provides multiple lines of statistical evidence showing that higher voriconazole MICs are associated with delayed clearance of T. marneffei in blood culture. Univariate analysis shows significant differences in MIC distributions between clearance groups, multivariate logistic regression identifies voriconazole MIC as an independent prognostic variable, and cumulative analysis demonstrates that clearance rates over time differ significantly by MIC group. This directly supports the inference that in vitro susceptibility to voriconazole independently influences treatment response.

343.Option: The high mortality rate of talaromycosis in some regions is primarily due to delayed diagnosis and limited access to antifungal drugs, rather than poor antifungal efficacy.
Evidence: However, the mortality of talaromycosis remains as high as 30% in some regions, representing an important public health problem threatening patients with AIDS [8,9]. The underlying mechanism of poor therapeutic effects in patients with AIDS complicated with talaromycosis is still unclear.

In this study, we analyzed the influencing factors of delayed clearance of T. marneffei in blood culture after two weeks’ antifungal therapy, including the clinical features and the antifungal susceptibilities of T. marneffei in vitro, by testing the minimal inhibition concentrations (MICs) of the major antifungal agents [10–12].
343.Rationale: The passage mentions high mortality but does not investigate its causes, such as delayed diagnosis or drug access. Instead, it focuses on factors affecting fungal clearance, specifically antifungal susceptibility. The statement that mortality is 'primarily due to delayed diagnosis and limited access' is not supported by any evidence in the passage; in fact, the study suggests that poor antifungal efficacy (linked to susceptibility) may contribute to poor outcomes, as it identifies voriconazole MIC as a factor influencing clearance.

343.Option: The elevated AST levels observed in the delayed clearance group are a direct result of voriconazole-induced hepatotoxicity, rather than a marker of systemic disease severity.
Evidence: While the Pos-group had a higher baseline Aspartate aminotransferase (AST, 78.5 vs. 105 U/L; P = 0.073) and lower CD4+ T cells level (11 vs. 7 cells/μl; P = 0.061) (shown in Table 1).
Multivariate analysis using the logistic regression model involving the 2 significant factors (AST and CD4+T levels)determined by univariate analysis and the MIC values for VOR and FLU.
343.Rationale: The passage reports elevated baseline AST in the delayed clearance group but does not investigate the cause of this elevation. It is described as a baseline characteristic, measured at admission before or early in treatment, not as an adverse event during therapy. There is no mention of voriconazole hepatotoxicity or any comparison of drug-related liver injury between groups. The inference that AST elevation is directly due to voriconazole is speculative and not supported by the passage; it could equally be a marker of systemic disease severity.

343.Option: The use of amphotericin B followed by itraconazole as first-line therapy is ineffective in patients with talaromycosis due to widespread resistance to these drugs.
Evidence: At present, amphotericin B (AMB) followed by itraconazole (ITRA) is the main treatment strategy for talaromycosis [5], and voriconazole (VOR) is regarded as an alternative in patients with AMB intolerance [6,7].
No differences were found in the distribution of MIC for POS, ITR, AMB, and echinocandins between the two groups.
343.Rationale: The passage states that AMB followed by ITRA is the main treatment strategy, implying it is considered effective. The antifungal susceptibility testing showed no significant differences in MIC distributions for AMB and ITRA between the clearance groups, indicating that resistance to these drugs is not associated with delayed clearance. Therefore, there is no evidence of widespread resistance rendering first-line therapy ineffective. The study's focus on voriconazole and fluconazole MICs as factors suggests that efficacy issues may relate to specific drugs, but not necessarily to first-line regimens.

344.Option: The Clone 13 vaccine is safe for use in pregnant ewes at any stage of gestation, as demonstrated by the absence of acute clinical signs in vaccinated animals.

as many as 7 lambs of 5 different ewes inoculated with Clone 13 at 50 days after mating showed malformations of the central nervous system such as hydranencephaly, hypoplasia of cerebrum, cerebellum and spinal cord and/or malformations of the musculoskeletal system

344.Option: Caution should be taken when the vaccine is used in pregnant animals, especially during the first trimester of pregnancy, as Clone 13 may spread to the fetus and cause malformations and stillbirths.
Evidence: Remarkably, inoculation during gestation resulted in vertical transmission of the virus to the fetus, which was associated with malformed lambs, stillbirths and precolostral antibodies.
Untoward events were most prominent in ewes inoculated during the first trimester of gestation.

The proportion of stillborn lambs was 30% for the group inoculated at approximately 50 days after mating (including the foetus that was about to be aborted) and 11% for the group inoculated at approximately 120 days after mating.

as many as 7 lambs of 5 different ewes inoculated with Clone 13 at 50 days after mating showed malformations of the central nervous system such as hydranencephaly, hypoplasia of cerebrum, cerebellum and spinal cord and/or malformations of the musculoskeletal system
344.Rationale: The experimental outcomes directly show that Clone 13 can cross the placental barrier and cause fetal harm, with more severe effects in the first trimester. This warrants caution in using the vaccine in pregnant animals, especially early in gestation.

344.Option: The teratogenic effects observed in the Clone 13 study are likely due to the NSs protein, which is known to mediate chromosome cohesion and segregation.
Evidence: Extensive studies have demonstrated that the NSs protein counteracts host innate immune responses at different levels, e.g. by suppression of type-I interferon responses, and is thereby considered the major virulence factor.
344.Rationale: The passage describes NSs as a virulence factor that suppresses interferon responses, but it does not attribute teratogenic effects to NSs or mention any role in chromosome cohesion and segregation. 344.Option: The Clone 13 vaccine does not cross the placental barrier, as no viral RNA was detected in the fetuses of ewes vaccinated at 120 days of gestation.
Evidence: Analysis of the lamb organs and plasma samples revealed that ten lambs were positive for Clone 13 RNA in the blood or one or more organ samples, six in the 50 days group and four in the 120 days group.

345.Option: The high diagnostic performance of chimeric recombinant antigens in this study suggests that they can fully replace all existing confirmatory tests for chronic Chagas disease, including those based on native parasite antigens.
Evidence: Based on AUC values, all IBMP chimeric antigens were classified as having outstanding diagnostic potential (Fig 2).

IBMP-8.1, IBMP-8.2 and IBMP-8.4 produced a sensitivity of 100%, while IBMP-8.3 offered 95%. All IBMP chimeric antigens attained the maximum specificity value of 100%, and maximum accuracy was achieved...
the high diagnostic performance offered by IBMP chimeric antigens and the need for confirmatory serological methods led us to perform a proof-of-concept study.

345.Option: The discontinuation of TESAcruzi production has created a critical gap in confirmatory testing for chronic Chagas disease, necessitating the development of alternative diagnostic platforms.
Evidence: Until 2015 and 2017, respectively, HBK 740 Immunoblot Linhas anti-T. cruzi and TESA-blot were routinely used for confirmatory testing [32]; however, commercial productions have since been discontinued, leaving a gap in the performance of confirmatory chronic CD diagnosis.
the need for confirmatory serological methods led us to perform a proof-of-concept study.

345.Option: The use of chimeric recombinant antigens in a Western blot platform offers a promising alternative to existing confirmatory tests for chronic Chagas disease, with high diagnostic performance and reduced risk of cross-reactivity.
Evidence: the high diagnostic performance offered by IBMP chimeric antigens and the need for confirmatory serological methods led us to perform a proof-of-concept study.
Based on AUC values, all IBMP chimeric antigens were classified as having outstanding diagnostic potential (Fig 2).

IBMP-8.1, IBMP-8.2 and IBMP-8.4 produced a sensitivity of 100%, while IBMP-8.3 offered 95%. All IBMP chimeric antigens attained the maximum specificity value of 100%, and maximum accuracy was achieved...
no cross-reactions were observed when assaying leishmaniasis-positive serum samples using all four chimeric proteins.

345.Option: The absence of cross-reactivity with visceral leishmaniasis in this study implies that chimeric recombinant antigens are universally safe for use in all co-endemic regions, regardless of local parasite diversity.
Evidence: no cross-reactions were observed when assaying leishmaniasis-positive serum samples using all four chimeric proteins.
