Evidence: Community members’ perceptions of the lymphedema management program focused more on the community awareness campaigns which occurred during the program as well as the distribution of the patient kits.
A 63 year old farmer in Manibandha described a theatrical awareness campaign: ‘Through Palla (Folk Dance) I learn how to keep the environment clean and use of mosquito net to prevent lymphedema.’

Patients generally emphasized that their communities were accepting of them and did not obstruct their participation in community events. They also noted increased support from the program and appreciation from their families and community members after they observed that the lymphedema management program was helping them: ‘The family and community members also appreciate the work of [the program]’ (74 year old male farmer, unilateral, stage 2).

A 41 year old male laborer (unilateral, stage 2) explained why he thought community members were open to the program: ‘There are no obstacles I faced from my family members or community members. [The program] is helping us so that my next generation will be free from these diseases.’
417.Rationale: While community awareness campaigns are mentioned as part of the program and contributed to knowledge, participant accounts attribute reduced stigma and increased inclusion more to the observed benefits of the program (e.g., reduced acute episodes, improved health) rather than identifying awareness campaigns as the primary drivers. Evidence shows that acceptance increased after community members saw the program helping patients, indicating that direct benefits played a key role.

417.Option: The program's success in reducing acute episodes and improving work productivity has led to a significant increase in household income for patients and their families.
Evidence: Patients credited the program with changing their lives through knowledge and skills development, decreased acute episodes, and increased work productivity.
A 71 year old farmer (unilateral, stage 2) said simply that he was now able to work.

Family members discussed the economic impact of lymphedema, such as: ‘during the [acute] attack the whole family suffers. We all have to look after the patient. Two to three persons have to be always with the patient leaving all their work behind’ (48 year old male farmer).

Another male family member (35 year old weaver) stated: ‘while my mother suffers from fever. It is very painful for our entire family …I am not able to concentrate in my work…. This is affecting my work a lot. Even economically I have become very poor’.
417.Rationale: The passage provides evidence that the program reduced acute episodes and improved work productivity, which likely alleviates economic burden. However, participant accounts do not explicitly mention a 'significant increase in household income.' The focus is on reducing work loss and economic strain, not on quantifying income changes. Therefore, this inference cannot be directly derived from the evidence presented.

417.Option: The community-based lymphedema management program has contributed to a reduction in stigma associated with lymphedema, as evidenced by increased social acceptance and inclusion of patients in community life.
Evidence: Since participating in the lymphedema management program, patients generally emphasized that their communities were accepting of them and did not obstruct their participation in community events.

A 41 year old male laborer (unilateral, stage 2) explained: ‘There are no obstacles I faced from my family members or community members. [The program] is helping us so that my next generation will be free from these diseases.’

Family members also commented on an improvement of attitudes towards patients who were previously stigmatized. A 45 year old businessman mentioned that ‘Previously people disliked lymphedema patients but now there is no discrimination, people are aware through television, radio about the disease.’

Community members in general insisted that discrimination against lymphedema patients did not exist and all the participants in one male focus group concurred: ‘There is no restriction for them to participate in any village function, they worship with us in the same temple, we bathe in a common pond’.
417.Rationale: Participant accounts directly indicate that after the program, stigma decreased, and patients experienced greater social acceptance and inclusion in community activities. This is explicitly stated by patients, family members, and community members, supporting the inference that the program contributed to reduced stigma.

417.Option: The program has led to a complete eradication of traditional beliefs about the causes of lymphedema in the community, replacing them entirely with biomedical explanations.
Evidence: Despite these responses, many patients and community members expounded other causes for ‘batajaro’. Nevertheless, alternative explanations were generally voiced alongside responses that emphasized program messages.

For example, claims about dietary, hereditary, or lifestyle causes and treatment of LF and lymphedema were common. This point is illustrated by the comments of one 74 year old farmer (unilateral, stage 2) who claimed rice water and dry fish caused the disease, but later went on to state that he had gotten LF because he had not slept under a mosquito net.
Participants in two of the four community member focus groups mentioned that lymphedema was hereditary, but no disagreement was voiced when others mentioned mosquito transmission causing LF.
With regard to treatment, other remedies, including homeopathic medication were mentioned, though most patients claimed it had not worked.
417.Rationale: The passage shows that traditional beliefs about causes (e.g., dietary, hereditary) persist alongside biomedical explanations from the program. There is no evidence of 'complete eradication'; instead, mixed beliefs coexist. Therefore, this inference is not supported by the evidence.

417.Option: The lymphedema management program has significantly improved the mental health of patients by eliminating feelings of isolation and emotional distress.
Evidence: Patients expressed feelings of social isolation living with lymphedema. As a 48 year old housewife (bilateral, stage 4) put it, ‘We feel bad when people call us as “GODARI” [Elephantiasis], and discuss our swelling legs mischievously.’

Although many patients said their families were supportive, an 80 year old farmer (unilateral, stage 2) divulged: ‘My family members don’t take meals with me. I have to eat separately. This is really hurting me’.

A few patients indicated resignation to continual suffering. One 40 year old housewife with stage six lymphedema stated that this was their destiny and a 62 year old male with bilateral stage six lymphedema expressed: ‘I am not able to do anything. My family members are asking when I will die’.
After the program, patients noted increased acceptance: ‘The family and community members also appreciate the work of [the program]’ (74 year old male farmer, unilateral, stage 2).
417.Rationale: While the program contributed to reduced isolation and improved social acceptance, participant accounts do not show that feelings of isolation and emotional distress were 'eliminated.' Some patients still expressed distress or resignation, and the evidence suggests improvement rather than complete elimination. Therefore, this inference is too strong and not directly derivable.

418.Option: The predominance of the Karp genotype of O. tsutsugamushi in Quang Nam province may explain the absence of rash in patients with scrub typhus, as this genotype is associated with lower rash frequency compared to other genotypes.
Evidence: The majority (9/14; 64.4%) of contemporary O. tsutsugamushi genotypes were related to the Karp strain.
No patient was observed to have a rash.
418.Rationale: While the passage confirms the predominance of the Karp genotype among sequenced strains and notes the absence of rash, it provides no evidence linking the Karp genotype to rash frequency or clinical presentation. The absence of such a correlation within the passage means this explanation cannot be derived through epidemiological reasoning from the provided text.

418.Option: The absence of rash in scrub typhus patients may be due to the relatively dark skin of Vietnamese individuals, which can make rashes difficult to detect clinically.
418.Rationale: The passage contains no information or discussion regarding skin color, skin tone, or any challenges in clinical detection of rashes related to pigmentation. Therefore, this explanation is not supported by any evidence within the passage and cannot be derived from it.

418.Option: The use of doxycycline and azithromycin in treatment may have suppressed the development of rash in patients with scrub typhus.
Evidence: Treatment with either doxycycline or azithromycin is still highly effective for both children and adults, although evidence of O. tsutsugamushi resistance to doxycycline has been reported in northern Thailand.
418.Rationale: The passage mentions the effectiveness of these antibiotics for treatment but provides no information on whether patients received antibiotic therapy prior to admission or before the clinical observation of rash. Without evidence linking pre-admission antibiotic use to the absence of rash, this explanation is speculative and not derivable from the passage.

418.Option: The absence of rash in scrub typhus patients may be due to the lack of serological confirmation in the acute phase, as IFA results may be negative early in infection.
Evidence: Serological evidence of infection generally appears in the second or third week of illness.
No patient was observed to have a rash.
418.Rationale: The passage notes the timing of serological positivity and the clinical absence of rash, but it does not establish a causal link between the two. The absence of rash is a clinical observation independent of diagnostic test results; some patients were confirmed by qPCR early in infection. Therefore, this explanation conflates diagnostic timing with clinical presentation and is not supported by the passage.

418.Option: The lack of rash in scrub typhus patients is likely due to delayed presentation to healthcare facilities, as most patients had been ill for over a week before admission.
Evidence: The median illness duration was one week, but some patients had been admitted more than 10 days after the fever.
No patient was observed to have a rash.
418.Rationale: Although the passage indicates that patients presented after a median of one week of illness, it provides no information on the typical temporal pattern of rash in scrub typhus (e.g., whether rash is early and transient). Without evidence linking delayed presentation to rash resolution, the claim that delayed presentation 'likely' explains the absence of rash is an assumption not derivable solely from the passage through epidemiological reasoning.

419.Option: The absence of M. perstans infection in Mahenge suggests that the parasite is not present in any onchocerciasis-endemic areas of sub-Saharan Africa, and therefore cannot be a co-factor in nodding syndrome development.
Evidence: None of the 113 epilepsy cases (including 43 with probable NS) and 132 controls were diagnosed with an M. perstans infection.
The infection is spread by midges (Culicoides spp.) and is believed to be prevalent in Western, Eastern and Central Africa and neotropical regions in South America [15].
419.Rationale: The passage reports no M. perstans infections in the Mahenge study population, but it explicitly states that M. perstans is believed to be prevalent in other parts of Africa. Therefore, the absence in Mahenge does not justify the broad conclusion that it is absent in all onchocerciasis-endemic areas or that it cannot be a co-factor elsewhere.

419.Option: The lack of onchocercal nodules in the study population indicates that ivermectin treatment has completely eliminated O. volvulus transmission in Mahenge, rendering the parasite no longer a risk factor for nodding syndrome.
Evidence: Onchocercal skin manifestations were only found in persons with epilepsy (6.2%, p = 0.0040), especially among probable NS cases (9.3%, p = 0.0033).
Ov16 seroprevalence was associated with epilepsy (OR: 2.03, 95% CI: 1.07–3.86), including OAE (OR: 2.26, 95% CI: 1.15–4.47) and probable NS (OR: 5.05, 95% CI: 1.79–14.27).

Skin snips were not obtained for O. volvulus microfilariae detection, as nearly all study participants took ivermectin during annual CDTI. The latter has been shown to mask the association between onchocerciasis and epilepsy [34].
419.Rationale: Onchocercal nodules were observed in persons with epilepsy, indicating that O. volvulus infection is still present. The significant association between Ov16 seropositivity and epilepsy/OAE/NS shows that the parasite remains a risk factor. Ivermectin treatment may reduce detectable microfilariae but does not eliminate transmission or risk, as evidenced by the ongoing associations.

419.Option: The association between M. perstans and nodding syndrome in South Sudan was likely due to shared poverty and farming practices, rather than a direct parasitic link, and therefore M. perstans should be considered a proxy for socioeconomic risk in endemic areas.
Evidence: In three case-control studies conducted between 2001 and 2002 in Mundri, Western Equatoria State in South Sudan, not only a positive association was found between NS and an O. volvulus infection (odds ratio (OR): 9.2, p-value (p) <0.001), but also between NS and M. perstans infection (OR: 3.2, p = 0.005) [13].
419.Rationale: The passage reports the statistical association between M. perstans and NS in South Sudan but does not mention poverty, farming practices, or socioeconomic factors as explanations. There is no evidence provided to support that the association is due to shared socioeconomic risks rather than a direct parasitic link. Therefore, this inference cannot be derived from the passage.

419.Option: The clustering of epilepsy within families in Mahenge is primarily due to genetic predisposition, as evidenced by the high prevalence of first-degree relatives with epilepsy among cases.
Evidence: Persons with epilepsy were also more likely to have a family member with epilepsy (p<0.0001).
having a first-degree relative with epilepsy was strongly associated with Ov16 seropositivity (adjusted OR: 15.02, 95% CI: 2.97–274.7; p = 0.0094).
Moreover, a family history of seizures lost all significance when an interaction effect with Ov16 positivity was added to the conditional logistic regression for epilepsy, OAE or NS.
419.Rationale: While family clustering of epilepsy is observed, the passage shows that family history is closely linked to O. volvulus exposure (Ov16 seropositivity). When accounting for this exposure, the significance of family history diminishes, indicating that shared environmental factors (like onchocerciasis infection) rather than genetic predisposition alone likely explain the clustering.

419.Option: Onchocerciasis remains the main risk factor for nodding syndrome and other forms of epilepsy that meet the criteria for onchocerciasis-associated epilepsy, despite the lack of a fully understood pathogenic mechanism.
Evidence: More recent epidemiological studies suggest that NS is one of the clinical manifestations of onchocerciasis-associated epilepsy (OAE) [3].
Ov16 seroprevalence was associated with epilepsy (OR: 2.03, 95% CI: 1.07–3.86), including OAE (OR: 2.26, 95% CI: 1.15–4.47) and probable NS (OR: 5.05, 95% CI: 1.79–14.27).
Onchocercal skin manifestations were only found in persons with epilepsy (6.2%, p = 0.0040), especially among probable NS cases (9.3%, p = 0.0033).

Despite a large number of epidemiological studies showing the association between onchocerciasis and epilepsy, there is a reluctance to consider this association causal, mainly because there is little evidence that O. volvulus microfilariae can penetrate the brain [10] and because, so far, no indirect mechanism has been identified to explain the pathogenesis of OAE [9].
419.Rationale: The passage presents strong epidemiological evidence linking onchocerciasis to NS and OAE, including significant odds ratios for Ov16 seropositivity and onchocercal skin manifestations in cases. It also acknowledges that the pathogenic mechanism is not fully understood, but the association persists. Therefore, it can be inferred that onchocerciasis is a key risk factor despite the lack of mechanistic clarity.

421.Option: The success of community-wide MDA programmes is primarily determined by the age group targeted, with school-aged children being the most effective group to treat for achieving transmission interruption.
Evidence: Published research indicates that the interruption of STH and schistosome transmission is unlikely to occur when only treating SAC and WRA [11].

Faced with the prospect of a never-ending cycle of treatment and re-infection, a developing body of evidence has led to suggestions that endemic countries should consider expanding MDA de-worming efforts beyond the current school-based treatment system towards community-wide control [24–26].
421.Rationale: The passage indicates that targeting only school-aged children (SAC) and women of reproductive age (WRA) is insufficient for transmission interruption, and community-wide treatment is recommended. The results from the Geshiyaro project focus on community-wide MDA, not exclusively on SAC. Therefore, the inference that SAC are the most effective group for achieving transmission interruption cannot be derived from the passage; instead, it suggests broadening treatment to all age groups.

421.Option: The non-compliance of a small group of individuals can significantly affect the achievement of transmission interruption goals in community-wide MDA programmes, even when overall treatment coverage is relatively high.
Evidence: There is also a growing number of studies exploring MDA acceptance within treated communities and evidence points towards untreated adult populations serving as a reservoir of infection, thus causing re-infection of treated and untreated individuals [11,14–16].

The analyses presented in the work of Hardwick and colleagues [30] demonstrates the use of a conditional probability model in stochastic individual-based simulations by running two example forecasts for the elimination of STH transmission employing MDA within the TUMIKIA trial setting with different adherence patterns. This suggested a substantial reduction in the probability of transmission elimination (between 23–43%) when comparing the observed adherence patterns which recorded partial non-compliance, with an assumption of randomness in who gets treated at each round with defined levels of coverage.

For fully non-compliant individuals (never treated) who did not partake in MDA during each of the previous years, no individual could be designated as uninfected based on the Kato Katz diagnostic method.

In individuals that had not received MDA at any round saw an increase in mean EPG from that calculated at baseline (T. trichiura; 422.11, 588.35, 704.2, A. lumbricoides; 1041.95, 1610.6, 1269.96 and Hookworm; 51.8, 62.33, 103.89).
421.Rationale: The passage provides evidence that untreated individuals serve as reservoirs of infection, leading to re-infection. Simulation studies show that non-compliance patterns reduce the probability of transmission elimination. Empirical data from the sentinel sites demonstrates that never-treated individuals maintain or increase infection intensity, hindering community-wide reduction. This supports the inference that non-compliance of a small group can significantly impact transmission interruption goals, even with overall coverage.

421.Option: The aggregation of infection in a small number of individuals is primarily driven by genetic predisposition, which makes targeted treatment of high-burden individuals ineffective in interrupting transmission.
Evidence: Helminth parasites are highly aggregated within human populations, with a small number of individuals harbouring the majority of worms within an infected community [19–22].

MDA could be focused on the individuals with the highest aggregates of parasites, with the suggestion that these individuals will show a greater propensity to shed infective eggs into the community [23]. This highly focused approach has not been used at present due to the high effort and cost associated with measuring the intensity of infection within the total population, compared to treating the target population without taking a stool sample. It may be a cost-effective option when overall prevalence is very low, and drugs are no longer donated without cost to MDA programmes.
421.Rationale: The passage mentions aggregation of infection but does not attribute it to genetic predisposition. It discusses focused treatment on high-burden individuals as potentially effective but not implemented due to practical constraints. There is no evidence that targeted treatment is ineffective; in fact, it is suggested as a cost-effective option in low-prevalence settings. Therefore, this inference cannot be derived from the passage.

421.Option: The Geshiyaro project’s use of biometric registration and study ID cards has led to a significant reduction in the overall prevalence of STH infections across all age groups in the sentinel sites.
Evidence: In the most recent year (Y2) of the longitudinal survey, prevalence of infection with any STH was 31.4%. compared with a 32.3% level at baseline.

There was considerable variation in trends across the four kebeles. Two kebeles had a lower prevalence of any STH in Y2 compared to BS (Afama Garo: 56.07% BS– 29.25% Y2, Korke Doge; 40.69% BS– 31.62%) and reductions in prevalence proved significant (P < 0.001) ... In two kebeles, Giddo Homba and Hajo Salata, prevalence of any STH increased year on year.
Biometric registration and study ID cards are used to register the individual uptake of MDA.
421.Rationale: The data shows minimal overall reduction in prevalence from baseline to Y2 (32.3% to 31.4%), which is not significant. While biometric registration enabled tracking of compliance, it is a tool for data collection, not an intervention causing reduction. Prevalence changes varied by kebele, with some increases. Therefore, the inference that biometric registration led to a significant reduction across all age groups is not supported by the evidence.

422.Option: The anti-viral activity of viperin against DENV-2 is mediated by a C-terminal interaction with the DENV-2 NS3 protein and does not require membrane association or lipid droplet localization.
Evidence: The interaction of viperin and NS3 but not membrane association, however, is necessary for viperins anti-viral actions.
C-terminal deletions, as small as 17 amino acids, completely abolished the anti-DENV-2 activity of viperin
Mutation of the single C-terminal residue of viperin (CTM) partly abrogated the anti-DENV-2 activity
FRET analysis also demonstrated an interaction of DENV-2 NS3 and WT-viperin
The ampipathic helix mutant (5'Δ33) of viperin, which retains its anti-DENV-2 activity, but has lost its membrane localisation ability
the C-terminal viperin mutant, 3'Δ17, which has no anti-DENV-2 activity ... showed no positive FRET with DENV-2 NS3
422.Rationale: The passage explicitly states that the interaction with NS3 is necessary for anti-viral action, while membrane association is not. Experimental data shows that C-terminal deletions abolish anti-DENV-2 activity, and FRET confirms interaction with NS3. The N-terminal mutant that loses membrane localization retains activity, supporting that lipid droplet localization is not required.

422.Option: Viperin restricts DENV-2 replication by disrupting lipid raft integrity, similar to its mechanism of action against influenza virus.
Evidence: alterations to lipid rafts (influenza, [14])
422.Rationale: While the passage mentions that viperin alters lipid rafts in influenza virus infection, there is no evidence presented that viperin disrupts lipid raft integrity in the context of DENV-2. The mechanism for DENV-2 involves C-terminal interaction with NS3 and does not require membrane association, as per the experimental findings.

422.Option: The induction of viperin in DENV-2-infected primary MDM is primarily driven by interferon signaling from infected cells, leading to a systemic antiviral state in bystander cells.
Evidence: viperin mRNA was not increased following DENV-2 infection of Huh-7.5 cells, a cell line which is defective in dsRNA signalling via a mutation in the pathogen-recognition receptor RIG-I
DENV-antigen negative bystander cells ... were shown to express significantly increased levels of viperin.
this up-regulation of viperin was again only observed in the DENV-2 antigen −ve bystander cells
422.Rationale: The passage shows that viperin induction requires RIG-I signaling and is observed in bystander cells, suggesting paracrine interferon signaling may be involved. However, it does not explicitly state that induction is 'primarily driven by interferon signaling from infected cells,' nor does it support a 'systemic antiviral state' (which implies organism-level effects, whereas the study is in vitro). The evidence indicates local upregulation in cell cultures but cannot support systemic inferences.

422.Option: Viperin inhibits DENV-2 replication by directly binding to and degrading viral RNA, as evidenced by co-precipitation of DENV-2 RNA with viperin.
Evidence: successful co-precipitation of DENV-2 RNA with FLAG-antibody
422.Rationale: Co-precipitation of DENV-2 RNA with viperin indicates association, but does not demonstrate direct binding or degradation. The passage states that viperin restricts DENV-2 by reducing viral RNA production through interaction with NS3, not by direct RNA degradation. No evidence for degradation is presented.

422.Option: The anti-viral effect of viperin against DENV-2 is dependent on its ability to co-localize with the DENV-2 capsid (CA) protein at lipid droplet interfaces.
Evidence: partial co-localisation between DENV-2 CA and viperin at the interface of lipid droplet-like structures
The N-terminal viperin deletion mutant (Vip5′Δ33) which loses its ability to localise to lipid droplets, but remains anti-viral against DENV-2
422.Rationale: While viperin co-localizes with CA at lipid droplets, the N-terminal mutant that cannot localize to lipid droplets retains anti-DENV-2 activity, demonstrating that co-localization with CA at lipid droplets is not required for the anti-viral effect. The essential interaction is with NS3, not CA.

423.Option: The lack of surveillance and diagnostic capacity for rabies detection means that data on disease burden are insufficient to motivate policy-makers, and impacts of control efforts cannot be evaluated.
Evidence: Hospital records of animal-bite injuries compiled from northwest Tanzania were used as primary data sources.

Rabies monitoring operations including passive and active surveillance involving veterinarians, village livestock field officers, paravets, rangers and scientists were used to collect samples from carcasses (domestic dogs and wildlife whenever found), which were subsequently tested and viral isolates were sequenced
Statistical techniques applied to these data for estimating epidemiological parameters and inferring transmission links are described elsewhere [10],[12].
423.Rationale: The passage describes active data collection and surveillance methods, including hospital records, contact tracing, and monitoring operations, which are used to estimate disease burden and evaluate control efforts. This indicates that surveillance and diagnostic capacity exist and are being utilized, contradicting the claim that data are insufficient to motivate policy-makers or evaluate impacts.

423.Option: The high cost of post-exposure prophylaxis (PEP) in Africa and Asia makes it economically unsustainable to rely on PEP alone for rabies prevention, necessitating a shift to mass dog vaccination campaigns.
423.Rationale: The passage does not mention post-exposure prophylaxis (PEP) or its cost. While it discusses mass dog vaccination campaigns and resource constraints, there is no information on PEP specifically. Therefore, this inference cannot be derived from the passage body.

423.Option: The persistence of rabies in wildlife populations across southern Africa suggests that canine rabies elimination through dog vaccination alone is not feasible in those regions.
Evidence: we conclude that the elimination of canine rabies is a feasible objective for much of Africa and there should be no reasons for further delay.
there are no insurmountable logistic, practical, epidemiological, ecological or economic obstacles.
423.Rationale: The passage explicitly states that elimination is feasible for much of Africa and that ecological obstacles, including wildlife reservoirs, are not insurmountable. This contradicts the inference that canine rabies elimination is not feasible due to wildlife persistence.

423.Option: The low R0 values observed in African dog populations indicate that rabies transmission is inherently weak, making it unlikely that any intervention would be needed to achieve elimination.
423.Rationale: The passage does not mention R0 values or the inherent strength of rabies transmission in African dog populations. It describes escalating incidence in Africa and Asia, suggesting ongoing transmission, but provides no data on reproduction numbers. Thus, this inference lacks support from the passage.

423.Option: The elimination of canine rabies through domestic dog vaccination is epidemiologically feasible in most of Africa, despite concerns about wildlife reservoirs and operational challenges.
Evidence: we conclude that the elimination of canine rabies is a feasible objective for much of Africa and there should be no reasons for further delay.
there are no insurmountable logistic, practical, epidemiological, ecological or economic obstacles.

we address each of these issues in turn, using outputs from modelling approaches and data from field studies to demonstrate that there are no insurmountable logistic, practical, epidemiological, ecological or economic obstacles.
423.Rationale: The passage directly states that elimination is feasible in much of Africa, based on epidemiological and operational data from modeling and field studies. It explicitly addresses concerns about constraints, including ecological challenges like wildlife reservoirs, and concludes they are not insurmountable.

424.Option: The observed variation in STH prevalence across sub-Divisions may be due to differences in the timing and coverage of community-based LF-MDA, which could have influenced local reinfection rates after treatment.
Evidence: termination of LF-MDA decisions in three Divisions of the country [6,7]: the final LF-MDA 7th round for the Western Division took place in 2009, and the 10th for the Central and 9th for the Northern in 2012.
Prevalence levels for any STH differed across sub-Divisions (P = 0.0014), with the highest frequency, up to 38.2% (95% CI: 17.8–63.8), in the Nataisiri sub-Division of the Central Division.
424.Rationale: The Passage Body provides information on the timing of LF-MDA in different Divisions and shows variation in STH prevalence across sub-Divisions. However, there is no evidence presented that links the observed STH prevalence variation to differences in LF-MDA timing and coverage. No analysis or correlation is provided to support this causal inference, making it not derivable from the Passage Body through epidemiological reasoning.

424.Option: The higher prevalence of Ascaris infections in younger children suggests that school-based deworming should be prioritized for class 1 and 2 students to reduce transmission across age groups.
Evidence: Prevalence levels of any STH and Ascaris infections were highest in class 1–2 students and lowest in class 7–8 students (P = 0.0216)
424.Rationale: While the Passage Body shows higher STH prevalence in younger children in the Eastern Division, the question specifically asks for strategic implications arising from variation in infection rates across different regions. This option focuses on age-based variation, not regional variation, and thus does not directly address the question's context. Therefore, it cannot be derived as the primary strategic implication from the observed regional variation.

424.Option: The use of LF-TAS as a platform for assessing STH epidemiology has been clearly demonstrated in other countries, and this approach is now recommended by WHO guidelines for all post-LF-MDA surveillance settings.
Evidence: an integrated approach within actions against neglected tropical diseases is strongly advocated by WHO [2].
424.Rationale: The Passage Body mentions that an integrated approach for NTDs is advocated by WHO, but it does not state that the use of LF-TAS for STH assessment has been clearly demonstrated in other countries or that it is recommended by WHO guidelines for all post-LF-MDA surveillance settings. This claim requires external knowledge and is not supported by specific evidence in the Passage Body.

424.Option: Given the varying levels of STH prevalence at the sub-national level, the control program can now consider applying preventive chemotherapy in any Division or sub-Division where the prevalence exceeds 20%.
Evidence: Prevalence levels for any STH differed across sub-Divisions (P = 0.0014), with the highest frequency, up to 38.2% (95% CI: 17.8–63.8), in the Nataisiri sub-Division of the Central Division.

the approach for STH has been mainly considered 'transmission control', based on providing preventive chemotherapy as a public health intervention for all young children, preschool and school-age children living in areas where the baseline prevalence of any STH is 20% or higher among children
424.Rationale: The Passage Body demonstrates significant variation in STH prevalence across sub-Divisions, with some areas like Nataisiri having prevalence above 20%. According to WHO guidelines cited in the passage, preventive chemotherapy is indicated for areas where baseline prevalence is 20% or higher. Therefore, a direct strategic implication from the observed regional variation is to target preventive chemotherapy to Divisions or sub-Divisions where prevalence exceeds 20%, aligning with established public health guidelines.

424.Option: Since the national STH prevalence is below 10%, the country should discontinue all preventive chemotherapy and shift focus entirely to WASH interventions.
Evidence: At the national level, the any STH prevalence among class 1 and 2 students assessed via KK smear was 10.5% (95% CI: 6.9–15.5)
Prevalence levels for any STH differed across sub-Divisions (P = 0.0014), with the highest frequency, up to 38.2% (95% CI: 17.8–63.8), in the Nataisiri sub-Division of the Central Division.
424.Rationale: While the national STH prevalence is approximately 10.5%, the Passage Body shows that several sub-Divisions have prevalence well above 20%, such as Nataisiri at 38.2%. WHO guidelines recommend preventive chemotherapy based on local prevalence, not national prevalence alone. Therefore, discontinuing all preventive chemotherapy is not supported, as some areas still require intervention. The evidence indicates that a targeted approach is needed rather than a complete shift.

425.Option: The significant heterogeneity observed in the meta-analysis is primarily due to differences in patient populations across studies, including ICU admission and underlying diseases.
Evidence: The result of meta regression (Table 2) indicated that sample types might be one possible source of heterogeneity.
425.Rationale: The passage identifies sample types as a possible source of heterogeneity but does not attribute it primarily to patient populations such as ICU admission or underlying diseases. While some studies involve ICU patients, the meta-regression points to sample types, not patient characteristics, as a key factor. Therefore, this inference cannot be derived from the passage.

425.Option: The presence of vanB genes in anaerobic bacteria that are naturally found in the human body may lead to false-negative results in GeneXpert vanA/vanB testing.
425.Rationale: The passage does not mention anaerobic bacteria or the natural presence of vanB genes in the human body. It discusses VRE testing in clinical specimens without addressing commensal flora as a cause of false negatives. Thus, this option relies on external biological knowledge not provided in the passage and cannot be derived from it.

425.Option: GeneXpert vanA/vanB testing is more accurate for vanB detection because it has a higher negative likelihood ratio, indicating strong ability to rule out vanB infection.

The inference that i.d. leads to a less effective response or greater damage is not supported by the evidence; both routes result in controlled, sublethal infection with similar resolution patterns.

434.Option: The sustained lung inflammation observed after the peak of bacterial load is likely due to ongoing bacterial replication in the pulmonary tissue, indicating incomplete pathogen clearance.
Evidence: Initially, the magnitude of lymphohistiocytic cellular infiltration coincided with increased bacterial loads; however, it continued to intensify as the bacterial loads were controlled.
Lung pathology during O. tsutsugamushi Gilliam strain infection progressed throughout the time course observed, and the onset of significant pathology scores correlated with the route of inoculation.
434.Rationale: The passage indicates that bacterial loads are controlled over time (e.g., 'as the bacterial loads were controlled'), and inflammation persists or intensifies independently. This suggests that sustained inflammation is immune-mediated rather than due to ongoing bacterial replication, so the inference that incomplete clearance is the cause is not supported by the temporal relationship.

435.Option: The significantly lower prevalence of IPIs in Sungai Layau village is due to the absence of domestic animals and the exclusive use of treated water, which together eliminate the primary transmission routes for intestinal parasites.
Evidence: Prevalence of IPIs were very high in most of the surveyed villages, ranging from 66.7% to 97.8%. Interestingly, infections were very low in Sungai Layau village (4.5%) (Table 1).

All villages were located at lowland altitude at the jungle fringes surrounded by rubber and palm oil estates. In general, although these communities have the provision of basic infrastructure (i.e., treated water and electricity) with concrete houses, these facilities are either not fully utilized or evenly distributed.

Even if these provisions were given, most of them could not afford to pay their monthly utility bills due to extreme poverty leading to the termination of water and electricity supplies. Therefore, rivers located adjacent to the village remained their main source of water for domestic needs (i.e., drinking, cooking, bathing and washing clothes).
Rearing of animals such as pigs, chickens, ducks, dogs, cats and monkeys are common practices.
435.Rationale: The passage mentions the low prevalence in Sungai Layau but does not provide village-specific data on the presence of domestic animals or exclusive use of treated water. The general description of villages indicates that treated water is often not utilized due to poverty, and rivers are the main water source. Animal rearing is described as a common practice across villages. There is no evidence that Sungai Layau differs in these aspects; thus, the claimed reasons for low prevalence are speculative and not derived from the passage.

435.Option: Restoring mass deworming programs alone is sufficient to control intestinal parasitic infections in rural West Malaysia, given the high re-infection rates observed after treatment.
Evidence: Not taking any anthelminthic drugs in the last 12 months (OR=1.29; 95% CI=1.01–1.65; p=0.038) was identified as a risk factor in univariate analysis.

The multivariate analysis using forward logistic regression model further confirmed that participant less than 12 years old were 2.2 times, low household income had 4.9 times, using untreated water supply had 2.1 times and indiscriminate defecation were 5 times more likely to suffer from an IPIs, respectively.
435.Rationale: The passage identifies multiple strong risk factors beyond anthelminthic drug use, including low income, untreated water, and indiscriminate defecation. While not taking anthelminthic drugs is associated with infection, the data show that socioeconomic and environmental factors have higher odds ratios. The passage is a cross-sectional study and does not provide data on re-infection rates after treatment. Therefore, the claim that deworming alone is sufficient is not supported; a comprehensive approach is more strongly indicated.

435.Option: The most effective way to reduce IPIs in rural West Malaysia is to focus on treating protozoan infections with targeted antiparasitic drugs, given their high prevalence and association with severe diarrhea in children.
Evidence: The overall prevalence of IPIs among 716 participants was 73.2% with STH infections (73.2%; 524) being significantly more common compared to protozoa infections (21.4%; 153) (p<0.001).

With regards to the protozoa infections, the overall prevalence was 21.4%. The highest prevalence rate was due to G. duodenalis (10.4%), followed by E. histolytica/dispar (10.2%) and Cryptosporidium sp. (2.1%).
435.Rationale: The data show that STH infections are far more prevalent than protozoan infections in this population. While protozoa can cause diarrhea, the passage does not provide specific data on the severity or burden of diarrhea in this study. The much higher prevalence of STH suggests that focusing solely on protozoa would neglect the majority of infections. Therefore, the claim is not supported by the evidence; a broader approach targeting all IPIs is needed.

435.Option: The high prevalence of T. trichiura infections in rural West Malaysia is primarily due to the widespread use of ineffective anthelminthic drugs and the development of drug resistance in the parasite population.
Evidence: The overall prevalence of STH infections was 73.2% with T. trichiura (66.8%) being the most predominant, followed by A. lumbricoides (38.5%) while only 12.8% had hookworm infections.
Not taking any anthelminthic drugs in the last 12 months (OR=1.29; 95% CI=1.01–1.65; p=0.038) was a risk factor in univariate analysis.
435.Rationale: The passage does not mention or provide any data on the effectiveness of anthelminthic drugs or drug resistance. The only reference to anthelminthic drugs is as a risk factor (not taking them), which suggests that taking drugs may reduce infection, not that drugs are ineffective. The high prevalence of T. trichiura is likely due to environmental and behavioral factors identified in the risk analysis, not drug resistance. Thus, this claim is not supported by the passage.

435.Option: Improvement of socioeconomic status, sanitation, health education to promote awareness about health and hygiene together with periodic mass deworming are better strategies to control these infections.
Evidence: These infections are ubiquitous with high prevalence among the poor and socioeconomically deprived communities where overcrowding, poor environmental sanitation, low level of education and lack of access to safe water are prevalent.

Multivariate analysis using forward logistic regression model further confirmed that participant less than 12 years old were 2.2 times (95% CI=1.45–3.45; p<0.001), low household income had 4.9 times (95% CI=3.15–7.73; p<0.001), using untreated water supply had 2.1 times (95% CI=1.36–3.21; p<0.001) and indiscriminate defecation were 5 times (95% CI=3.30–7.62; p<0.001) more likely to suffer from an IPIs, respectively.
Not taking any anthelminthic drugs in the last 12 months (OR=1.29; 95% CI=1.01–1.65; p=0.038) was also a risk factor in univariate analysis.
The establishment of such data will be beneficial for the public health service to justify and facilitate the reassessment of control strategies and policies.
435.Rationale: The passage identifies low socioeconomic status (income), poor sanitation (untreated water, indiscriminate defecation), and lack of anthelminthic treatment as key risk factors for IPIs. The background also highlights the association with poverty, poor sanitation, and low education. These findings strongly support a comprehensive approach that addresses socioeconomic determinants, improves sanitation and hygiene through education, and includes periodic deworming. This multi-pronged strategy is directly derivable from the evidence presented.

436.Option: The improvement in passive surveillance in 2015 likely led to a higher number of detected cases, which may have masked the true impact of vector control on reducing transmission.
Evidence: In 2015 surveillance was enhanced in the Mandoul region by improving the capacity for passive screening through equipping health facilities with rapid diagnostic tests (RDTs) and upgrading confirmation and treatment centres [17].
In 2015, the new passive strategy was predicted to result in slightly more case reporting than the basic medical strategy and so was not attributed to this particular decline.
436.Rationale: The passage indicates that enhanced passive surveillance in 2015 was accounted for in the modelling, and it did not mask the impact of vector control; rather, the model attributed the decline primarily to vector control, with the passive strategy not contributing significantly to the observed decline in cases.

436.Option: The addition of tsetse control to the 'screen and treat' strategy was responsible for the majority of the decline in new HAT infections in the Mandoul focus, with 70.4% of the two-year decline attributed to vector control.
Evidence: Modelling results indicate that 29.6% (95% CI: 5–49%) of this two-year decline was through active screening, and the remaining 70.4% (95% CI: 51–95%) was due to vector control.
436.Rationale: This statement is directly supported by the modelling results in the passage, which explicitly attribute 70.4% of the two-year decline in reported cases to vector control, indicating it was the majority contributor to the decline.

436.Option: The rapid and drastic decrease in catch of tsetse suggests that tiny targets can control G. f. fuscipes in this setting, and this success may be due to the small size of the area, well-defined habitat, and low reinvasion pressure.
Evidence: At the first evaluation post target deployment (T1, 2 months later), only 2 tsetse were caught (0.02 tsetse/trap/day) were observed.
the actual area on which targets were deployed represents 45 km2, corresponding to the gallery surrounding the portion of the Mandoul River where tsetse were found.
436.Rationale: The passage provides evidence for the rapid decrease in tsetse catch and describes the area as small and well-defined, but it does not mention or provide evidence for low reinvasion pressure. Therefore, the inference about low reinvasion pressure as a reason for success cannot be derived from the passage body.

437.Option: The age-related increase in CCHFV IgG antibody prevalence is likely due to the use of a non-specific ELISA assay that produces false positives in older animals.
Evidence: The camel-specific in-house CCHFV IgG ELISA was validated using 42 camel sera from Australia as negative controls.
Bland-Altman analysis [20] indicated good repeatability of the assay.

All samples were tested according to a flow chart [22] combining different ELISA and IFA test systems. The sera from cattle, sheep and goats were assayed (twice in case of a positive result) with the respective species-specific in-house CCHFV IgG ELISA system [23,24]. Positive findings were tested during a second test run with adapted commercial ELISAs (Vector Best, Novosibirsk, Russia) to confirm the results. In case of divergent results, an adapted commercial IFA (Euroimmun, Lübeck, Germany) was used to obtain a final result.
437.Rationale: The passage provides detailed validation and confirmation steps for the ELISA assays used, indicating they are specific and reliable. There is no evidence suggesting non-specificity or false positives in older animals. Instead, the assays were validated with negative controls and showed good repeatability, contradicting the option's claim.

437.Option: The higher seroprevalence in older animals is due to increased tick infestation rates in older livestock, which are more susceptible to vector-borne transmission.
Evidence: Investigations of cattle in Sudan [13] showed that collection site, age, husbandry system and tick infestation are the biggest risk factors for CCHFV seroprevalence.
437.Rationale: While the passage mentions tick infestation as a risk factor for CCHFV seroprevalence, it does not provide any direct evidence that older animals have higher tick infestation rates. The link between age and tick infestation is not established in the passage; the age-related increase is observed, but the mechanism is not specified. Thus, this option requires an assumption not derivable from the passage.

437.Option: The higher seroprevalence of CCHFV IgG antibodies in older animals across all species, combined with the lack of experimental data on antibody persistence, suggests that cumulative exposure to infected ticks over time is the most likely explanation for the observed age-related increase in seroprevalence.
Evidence: Moreover, we observed that older animals were more likely to be positive than younger animals (Table 3 and Fig 2A).
A more detailed statistical analysis revealed a significant effect of age and species on the CCHFV status of the investigated animals, whereas sex had no influence.
In addition, an age-related increase in CCHFV IgG antibody prevalence was already also observed in sheep [14] and cattle [13,15,16].
437.Rationale: The passage explicitly documents an age-related increase in CCHFV IgG antibody prevalence across multiple livestock species. Epidemiological reasoning suggests that for endemic infectious diseases transmitted by vectors, such as CCHFV via ticks, cumulative exposure over time is a common explanation for increasing seroprevalence with age, especially in the absence of data on antibody persistence (which is not discussed in the passage). This inference is directly supported by the observed patterns and aligns with standard epidemiological principles.

437.Option: The higher seroprevalence of CCHFV IgG antibodies in older animals is primarily due to longer-lasting antibody persistence in cattle and camels compared to sheep and goats.
437.Rationale: The passage does not provide any information about the duration or persistence of CCHFV IgG antibodies in different species. It reports seroprevalence rates and statistical effects but offers no data on antibody kinetics. Therefore, this explanation cannot be derived from the passage and is speculative.

437.Option: The observed age-related increase in CCHFV IgG antibody prevalence is mainly driven by differences in slaughter age, as young animals are more likely to be slaughtered before reaching high seroprevalence levels.
437.Rationale: The passage mentions sampling from slaughterhouses and markets but does not discuss slaughter age patterns or how they might influence seroprevalence. There is no evidence provided about the age at which animals are typically slaughtered or any bias in the sample due to slaughter practices. Thus, this option is an assumption not supported by the passage.

438.Option: The political commitment demonstrated by the establishment of the National Program for Blindness Control in 1989 laid the foundation for the long-term success of trachoma elimination in Togo.
Evidence: The organization of trachoma control began within the National Program for Blindness control created in 1989 by the Ministry of Health.
438.Rationale: The passage mentions the establishment of the National Program for Blindness Control in 1989 as the start of organized trachoma control. While this demonstrates political commitment and foundational efforts, it is not the key development that directly enabled Togo to meet the specific international criteria for elimination. Meeting the criteria requires evidence of reduced prevalence below WHO thresholds, which came from later surveys and interventions, not merely the establishment of a program.

438.Option: The 2017 survey, conducted according to the WHO recommended methodology, revealed that the prevalence of key indicators was below the WHO elimination threshold for trachoma, which allowed Togo to be validated as having eliminated trachoma as a public health problem.
438.Rationale: The passage does not mention a survey in 2017 conducted according to WHO methodology that revealed prevalence below elimination thresholds. It describes a Tropical Data survey using WHO methodology that showed TF prevalence <1% and low TT prevalence, but does not specify the year of this survey. The only reference to 2017 is mass treatment data giving a TT prevalence of 2.2%, which is not a formal survey and does not address TF prevalence. Therefore, the option is not supported by the passage.

438.Option: The use of the Tropical Data protocol in the 2017 survey allowed for the accurate identification of trichiasis cases, which led to the successful surgical management of all identified patients.
Evidence: The survey carried out in the 7 districts according to the Tropical Data protocol recommended by the WHO revealed a prevalence of TF of less than 1% in all districts among children aged 1 to 9 years and a very low prevalence of TT among 15-year-olds.
At the end of the survey, 27 patients were identified in need for surgery. 19 of them were surgered while 6 refused surgery and therefore benefited from epilation of eyelashes.
438.Rationale: The passage confirms the use of the Tropical Data protocol for a survey that identified trichiasis cases. However, it does not state that this survey was in 2017. Moreover, the survey did not lead to successful surgical management of all identified patients, as only 19 out of 27 received surgery, while others refused or were absent. Additionally, the key development for meeting elimination criteria is the demonstration of low prevalence, not just surgical management.

438.Option: The integration of trachoma screening into annual door-to-door mass treatment campaigns for onchocerciasis, schistosomiasis, and geo-helminthiasis enabled the identification of suspected trichiasis cases across all regions of Togo, including previously unsurveyed districts.
Evidence: Detection of suspected TT cases during mass treatment from 2015 to 2017.
As part of the mass treatment activities conducted door-to-door in all regions of the country, Community Health Workers were trained to identify suspected cases of trichiasis.
438.Rationale: The passage describes the integration of trachoma screening into mass treatment campaigns, which enabled identification of suspected trichiasis cases nationwide. However, this intervention alone did not directly enable Togo to meet the international criteria for elimination. The criteria require demonstration that TF prevalence is below 5% in children aged 1–9, which was provided by the Tropical Data survey, not solely by case identification. While integration contributed to surveillance and case management, it is not the key development that furnished the evidence needed for validation.

438.Option: The correction of methodological weaknesses in earlier surveys, such as the overdiagnosis of trachomatous follicular inflammation in 2006 and 2009, enabled the 2017 survey to produce reliable data for elimination validation.
438.Rationale: The passage presents results from earlier surveys (2006, 2009, 2011) without any mention of methodological weaknesses, overdiagnosis, or corrections. It simply reports the prevalence findings. Therefore, there is no evidence to support this claim.

440.Option: The higher total provider costs for P. vivax malaria are due to the greater number of outpatient consultations required for its treatment compared to P. falciparum.
Evidence: For P. vivax cases, one extra outpatient consultation was considered for radical cure six months after delivery.

The average cost of managing an outpatient case (multiple consultations) was US$ 100.19, and discriminating by Plasmodium species, it was US$ 103.51 for a P. vivax malaria episode and US$ 83.59 for a P. falciparum malaria episode.
Total provider costs for all malaria cases in pregnant women reported in Manaus in 2010 were US$ 17,038.50, of which US$ 15,741.14 (92.4%) was due to P. vivax malaria.
440.Rationale: While P. vivax requires an extra consultation per episode, the passage shows that higher total provider costs are primarily driven by the greater number of P. vivax cases, not solely by the number of consultations per episode. The evidence indicates that P. vivax accounts for 92.4% of total costs due to its higher prevalence, with unit costs being only slightly higher for P. vivax. The inference attributing higher total costs to more consultations per episode oversimplifies the data and is not directly supported.

440.Option: The economic burden of P. vivax malaria in pregnancy is higher than that of P. falciparum, particularly when multiple episodes are considered, despite P. vivax being the predominant species in the region.
Evidence: Total provider costs for all malaria cases in pregnant women reported in Manaus in 2010 were US$ 17,038.50, of which US$ 15,741.14 (92.4%) was due to P. vivax malaria.
Interestingly, all women experiencing two episodes during the study period had P. vivax malaria.

Median costs were US$ 259.50 (IQR, 120.69, 398.31) when the first malaria episode required admission and the second one was an outpatient consultation; median costs were US$ 25.38 (IQR, 22.79, 68.58) when the two malaria episodes were outpatient consultations.
440.Rationale: The evidence demonstrates that P. vivax accounts for the vast majority of provider costs (92.4%) due to its high prevalence. Additionally, all recurrent episodes in the study were caused by P. vivax, leading to significant cumulative patient costs. This supports the inference that P. vivax imposes a higher economic burden than P. falciparum in this setting, especially when considering multiple infections.

440.Option: The high cost of managing inpatient cases of malaria in pregnancy is primarily driven by the need for prolonged hospital stays and intensive care, rather than by the species of Plasmodium involved.
Evidence: The cost of one bed/day was US$ 118.51 (ranging from US$ 111.64 to US$ 124.57) and considering the whole length of stay the cost of management of an inpatient case was US$ 355.53 (ranging from US$ 334.92 to US$ 373.71).
An inpatient malaria episode requires hospital admission for 3 days plus two follow-up consultations as outpatients after discharge.
440.Rationale: The passage specifies a standard 3-day admission for inpatient cases, not prolonged stays. There is no mention of intensive care being required or driving costs. All admissions in the study were for P. vivax, so the influence of species on inpatient costs cannot be assessed, and the passage does not provide evidence on what primarily drives high costs. Thus, this inference cannot be derived.

440.Option: The economic burden of P. falciparum malaria in pregnancy is underestimated in this study due to its lower prevalence and the fact that it is more likely to be misdiagnosed as P. vivax.
Evidence: Pregnant and postpartum women ... diagnosed with clinical malaria (due to any Plasmodium species) microscopically confirmed
Plasmodium vivax is the predominant species, responsible for 84% of malaria cases
440.Rationale: The passage states that diagnosis is based on microscopic confirmation, which minimizes misdiagnosis. There is no evidence that P. falciparum is misdiagnosed as P. vivax. While P. falciparum has lower prevalence in the region, the study does not suggest that its economic burden is underestimated; costs are calculated based on confirmed cases. Therefore, this inference is not supported by the passage.

440.Option: The use of preventive measures such as bed nets and insecticides is associated with lower treatment costs for P. vivax malaria in pregnant women, suggesting that prevention is cost-effective.
Evidence: The reported use of bed nets by study women was low (6%; 4/73)
The use of insecticides and skin repellents was reported in 26% (19/73) and 19% (14/73) of interviews, respectively.
440.Rationale: The passage reports the frequency of preventive measure use but does not provide any data or analysis linking their use to treatment costs. There is no comparison of costs between women who used preventive measures and those who did not, nor is there any assessment of cost-effectiveness. Therefore, this inference cannot be derived from the evidence presented.

441.Option: NF2839’s ability to impair egg maturation without reducing egg production indicates that it disrupts developmental signaling pathways independent of parasite viability.
441.Rationale: The passage body does not mention the compound NF2839, nor does it report any experimental findings related to egg maturation or production for specific compounds. The methods describe egg counting and maturation scoring, but no results are provided. Therefore, this inference cannot be derived from the passage.

441.Option: The quinolone-based HDAC inhibitor NF2839 demonstrates schistosomicidal activity against both larval and adult stages of S. mansoni, impairs egg maturation, and increases acetylation of histones and tubulin in parasites, indicating it targets multiple aspects of parasite biology.
441.Rationale: The passage body does not mention NF2839 or any quinolone-based HDAC inhibitor. While it describes assays for viability, egg counts, and acetylation, no experimental results for specific compounds are reported. Thus, this inference lacks evidence from the passage.

441.Option: The quinolone-based HDAC inhibitor NF2839 shows selective inhibition of SmHDAC8 over other HDAC isoforms, which explains its potent schistosomicidal activity and specific impact on egg maturation.
441.Rationale: NF2839 is not mentioned in the passage body. The passage discusses SmHDAC8 as a potential target and methods for inhibition assays, but no findings on compound selectivity or activity are provided. Hence, this inference cannot be derived.

441.Option: The failure of NF2838 to affect parasite viability despite its structural similarity to NF2839 suggests that the quinolone-based cap group is not essential for schistosomicidal activity.
441.Rationale: The passage body does not mention NF2838, NF2839, or any structural comparisons between compounds. No experimental results on viability failures are reported. Therefore, this inference is not supported by the passage.

441.Option: The observed increase in histone and tubulin acetylation in NF2839-treated parasites is primarily due to the compound’s ability to inhibit hHDAC6, which is highly expressed in S. mansoni.
441.Rationale: NF2839 is not mentioned in the passage. The passage discusses hHDAC8 as poorly expressed in human cells, but does not mention hHDAC6 in the context of S. mansoni or compound effects. No experimental findings on acetylation increases are reported. Thus, this inference cannot be derived from the passage.

442.Option: The failure to systematically diagnose T. cruzi infection is one of the main causes of persistent neglect towards this global public health problem, and most patients remain undiagnosed and untreated.
Evidence: Chagas disease, one of 20 conditions classified as neglected tropical diseases (NTDs) by the World Health Organization (WHO) [1], affects more than 6 million people worldwide, mainly in endemic areas of Latin America, and is largely underdiagnosed.

According to estimates, less than 10% of those currently infected know their infection status [2], which represents a significant barrier to receive timely comprehensive care. Less than 1% of the estimated population with the infection receives etiological treatment.

In Colombia, Cucunubá and colleagues (2017) found that only 1.2% of the population considered at risk by the WHO [8] had been screened for Chagas disease, and only 0.4% of an estimated 438,000 infected persons had access to etiological treatment.
442.Rationale: The passage directly provides evidence that Chagas disease is underdiagnosed and that most infected individuals are unaware and untreated. This pattern results from limitations in test accuracy (e.g., variability in RDT performance), clinical decision support (e.g., need for confirmatory testing, unfamiliarity among healthcare providers), and infrastructure (e.g., lack of access to laboratory-based tests). Thus, the failure to systematically diagnose is a key cause of the disease's neglected status, directly supporting the overarching public health challenge described in the question.

442.Option: The use of rapid diagnostic tests (RDTs) in combination could provide diagnostic confirmation of chronic infection at the point of care, potentially improving access to early diagnosis and treatment for vulnerable populations.
Evidence: rapid tests are of great value in public health, as they can facilitate access to diagnosis, and enable case management in resource-limited and point of care (POC) settings.
to model potential combinations of RDTs for screening and confirmatory diagnosis of T. cruzi infection.
442.Rationale: While the passage supports the potential of RDTs and their combinations to improve diagnosis and access, this option describes a potential solution rather than the overarching public health challenge. The question specifically asks for the challenge supported by the pattern of failure due to limitations in test accuracy, clinical decision support, and infrastructure. Therefore, this option does not address the challenge but rather a strategy to mitigate it.

442.Option: Geographic variation in the performance of diagnostic tools, particularly due to differences in circulating DTUs, may limit the generalizability of rapid test results across Chagas disease-endemic regions.
Evidence: Such variability could be partially explained by genetic variation in T. cruzi, leading to differences in infection pathogenicity and transmission. Seven genetic lineages of the pathogen (discrete typing units, DTUs) have been identified to date, with different geographical distributions [17]. TcI is most common in human infections in Colombia [18].
442.Rationale: The passage mentions genetic variation as a possible factor in test performance variability, but it does not directly support this as the overarching public health challenge. The pattern of failure described in the question is more directly attributed to systemic barriers like limited access to confirmatory testing, poor infrastructure, and test accuracy issues, rather than geographic variation per se. The study was conducted only in Colombia, so evidence for geographic generalizability limitations is not strong in the passage.

442.Option: The high rate of discordant results between rapid tests and the reference standard, particularly false negatives, suggests that current rapid tests are unreliable for confirming chronic T. cruzi infection in clinical practice.
Evidence: Of the 6,401 tests analyzed, 7.4% (471) disagreed with the reference standard, of which 75.8% were false negatives and 24.2% (114) were false positives.

38.6% (226) of the samples had at least one discordant RDT result; 61.5% (139) were in samples previously characterized as positive and 38.5% (87) as negative, i.e., most of the discordant results were false negatives.
442.Rationale: Although the passage reports discordant results and false negatives, it also shows that many RDTs have high sensitivity and specificity (e.g., up to 99% sensitivity and 100% specificity). The study aims to identify the best-performing RDTs for further field testing, not to conclude that all rapid tests are unreliable. The pattern of failure includes broader systemic issues beyond test unreliability, such as infrastructure and decision support limitations. Therefore, this option overgeneralizes and does not fully capture the overarching challenge.

443.Option: The high seropositivity among dogs and equids in certain countries suggests that these animals can serve as useful sentinels for West Nile virus surveillance.
Evidence: Dogs (Djibouti, total SS = 91, prevalence = 56.5%; Morocco, total SS = 231, prevalence = 54–96%)

Equids (Iran, total SS = 1839, prevalence = 0.8–70.3%; Jordan, total SS = 253, prevalence = 24.9%; Morocco, total SS = 1189, prevalence = 25–100%; Pakistan, total SS = 449, prevalence = 65%; Palestine, total SS = 585, prevalence = 75%; Qatar, total SS = 421, prevalence: 0–27%; Saudi Arabia, total SS = 63, prevalence = 33.5%; Tunisia, total SS = 1473, prevalence = 28.0–45.2%; the UAE, total SS = 750, prevalence = 5.4–28.6%)
The prevention and control efforts substantially rely on effective surveillance of the infection in birds, vectors, animals, and humans.
Equines and humans are incidental “dead-end” hosts who do not play a role in the transmission cycle of the virus.
443.Rationale: The passage reports high seroprevalence in dogs and equids across multiple EMRO countries, indicating significant exposure to WNV. Given that these animals are dead-end hosts (like equines and humans), their infection reflects local virus circulation via mosquitoes. The passage also emphasizes that surveillance relies on monitoring animals. Thus, high seropositivity logically supports their utility as sentinels for detecting WNV activity in the region.

443.Option: The high seroprevalence of West Nile virus in dogs and equids across the EMRO region indicates that current surveillance systems are effectively detecting animal infections and informing public health responses.
Evidence: Understanding the epidemiology of WNV in the EMRO faces a number of challenges including inadequate knowledge of physicians about the nature of the disease, misdiagnosis of other common infectious diseases due to similarity in clinical presentations, poor diagnostic infrastructures and the absence of confirmatory assays for serological tests, and lack of a comprehensive and progressive monitoring and surveillance system in majority of countries.
The latter has resulted in a gap in knowledge regarding the prevalence of WNV infection in the EMRO region.
443.Rationale: The passage explicitly states that surveillance systems in the EMRO region are lacking or inadequate, leading to knowledge gaps. The high seroprevalence data come from individual studies synthesized in this systematic review, not from an effective ongoing surveillance system. In fact, the review was conducted to address the lack of comprehensive surveillance. Therefore, the inference contradicts the passage's description of surveillance challenges.

443.Option: The presence of West Nile virus in dogs and equids in multiple EMRO countries suggests that these animals are primary reservoirs responsible for maintaining the virus in the region.
Evidence: In nature, WNV is maintained in a zoonotic transmission cycle between birds and mosquitos, principally the Culex species.
Equines and humans are incidental “dead-end” hosts who do not play a role in the transmission cycle of the virus.
443.Rationale: The passage clearly identifies birds as the primary reservoir hosts in the WNV transmission cycle, with mosquitoes as vectors. Equines (and by extension, dogs as mammals) are described as incidental dead-end hosts that do not contribute to virus transmission. Therefore, the presence of WNV in dogs and equids does not indicate they are reservoirs; it only reflects spillover infection from the bird-mosquito cycle.

443.Option: The temporal pattern of West Nile virus seroconversion in dogs suggests that human infections in the EMRO region are primarily driven by direct contact with infected dogs.
Evidence: This mosquito-borne virus has been classified in the genus Flavivirus within the family Flaviviridae.
In nature, WNV is maintained in a zoonotic transmission cycle between birds and mosquitos, principally the Culex species.
443.Rationale: The passage describes WNV as mosquito-borne, with transmission occurring via mosquitoes between birds and incidentally to mammals. There is no mention of direct contact transmission from dogs to humans. Moreover, the passage does not discuss temporal patterns of seroconversion in dogs. Thus, the inference is unsupported and contradicted by the known transmission cycle described in the passage.

443.Option: The high seropositivity among dogs and equids in certain countries indicates that these animals are more susceptible to West Nile virus infection than other domestic animals in the EMRO region.
Evidence: Dogs (Djibouti, total SS = 91, prevalence = 56.5%; Morocco, total SS = 231, prevalence = 54–96%)

Equids (Iran, total SS = 1839, prevalence = 0.8–70.3%; Jordan, total SS = 253, prevalence = 24.9%; Morocco, total SS = 1189, prevalence = 25–100%; Pakistan, total SS = 449, prevalence = 65%; Palestine, total SS = 585, prevalence = 75%; Qatar, total SS = 421, prevalence: 0–27%; Saudi Arabia, total SS = 63, prevalence = 33.5%; Tunisia, total SS = 1473, prevalence = 28.0–45.2%; the UAE, total SS = 750, prevalence = 5.4–28.6%)
Camels (Morocco, total SS = 2775, prevalence = 8–23%; Palestine, total SS = 35, Prevalence = 40%)
Goats and sheep (Pakistan, total SS = 94, prevalence = 23.9%; Palestine, total SS = 95, prevalence = 14.7%)
Cows (Pakistan, total SS = 45, prevalence = 2.2%)

among patients who died, 80.9% were aged ≥ 40 years, and among those who were alive, 55.0% had the same age (p = 0.045).
Tetanus patients aged ≥40 years were 4.03 times (95% CI 1.07–15.17, p = 0.039) more likely to die due to tetanus than those aged <40 years.

It has a high case fatality rate, with an estimated 45.5% (95% CI: 43.7%-47.2%) deaths in African countries [4]. However, it might vary based on the availability and accessibility of well-equipped intensive care units.

453.Option: The effectiveness of Wolbachia-based arbovirus control might be compromised in ecosystems that experience periods of extreme heat, but given that Wolbachia levels partially recover after temperatures return to normal, any effects may be temporary.

Thus, this conclusion cannot be directly derived from the passage.

456.Option: The risk score performed well in discriminating case from control households with an accuracy of 0.78 and a sensitivity and specificity at a point value of 3 of 80% and 60%, respectively, suggesting that it can be used to predict individual-level infection risk with high confidence.

A risk score was calculated for each household.

456.Option: The presence of un-plastered exterior wall surfaces was significantly associated with case households, and is a direct proxy for rodent infestation, indicating that this feature should be prioritized in rodent control programs.

456.Option: Although our study focused on a single area within Salvador, it would be useful to assess the utility of our measure to predict risk of leptospiral infection in other slum areas, but this will require some tailoring of the survey instrument to some other locations, as every slum has unique characteristics and significant socioeconomic and environmental heterogeneities.

457.Option: The presence of the same haplotypes dominating the population in multiple fetuses and fetal brains from multiple dams suggests that these variants were likely acquired through random chance rather than selection.
Evidence: The presence of the same haplotypes dominating the population in multiple fetuses and fetal brains from multiple dams suggests these haplotypes may be the result of positive selection rather than purifying selection or genetic drift. However, if the variants comprising the haplotypes were present in the starting population at high frequencies, it likely would be the result of random chance.

The increase in frequency from the placenta to the fetal body to the fetal brain suggests that these variants may have been selected for in the fetal tissues, however we cannot definitively make this conclusion.
457.Rationale: The passage discusses both random chance and selection as possible explanations for the observed haplotypes. It does not conclude that random chance is the likely explanation; instead, it suggests that for some haplotypes present at high frequencies in the placenta, random chance may explain their dominance, while for others showing increased frequency from placenta to fetal tissues, selection may be at play. Thus, the inference that variants were 'likely acquired through random chance rather than selection' is not definitively supported by the passage.

457.Option: The observed reduction in ZIKV diversity in both the fetal bodies and fetal brains at the late stages of ZIKV infection could arise by the reduction in ZIKV diversity first occurring in the fetal periphery prior to neuroinvasion, or by the selection of certain ZIKV variants during entry into the brain.

The increase in frequency from the placenta to the fetal body to the fetal brain suggests that these variants may have been selected for in the fetal tissues
The dissimilarity in these two fetuses between their body and brain ZIKV populations is surprising considering the other eight fetal bodies and brains have identical dominant variants.
457.Rationale: The passage clearly documents a sharp reduction in ZIKV diversity in fetal bodies and brains at late infection stages. It suggests that selection pressures within the fetus could lead to homogeneous populations, and the increase in variant frequency from placenta to fetal tissues indicates possible selection. Additionally, the mismatched populations in two fetuses imply that selection might occur specifically during neuroinvasion. Thus, the inference that diversity reduction could arise either in the fetal periphery first or during brain entry is logically derived from the evidence.

457.Option: The immunocompromised nature of the AIR mouse model may influence the dynamics of vertical transmission, and the observed ZIKV population patterns may not reflect the selection pressures in immunocompetent humans.

pregnant anti-IFNAR1 treated Rag1-/- (AIR) mice

Thus, the inference that vertical transmission failed is contradicted by the evidence of viral RNA presence.

457.Option: The sharp reduction in ZIKV diversity in fetal bodies and brains is likely due to genetic drift rather than selection, as the fetal environment lacks strong immune pressure.
